entries.definition: "A Diamond Is Forever" is the De Beers advertising slogan written by copywriter Frances Gerety in 1947, later named by Advertising Age the top advertising slogan of the twentieth century.
entries.body:
The line was written by Frances Gerety, a copywriter at the Philadelphia agency N.W. Ayer, in 1947, for the De Beers account. In 1999, Advertising Age named it the number one advertising slogan of the twentieth century. Its staying power comes from a simple pairing: a diamond is the hardest natural material, a 10 on the Mohs scale, so "forever" reads as a metaphor for a commitment meant to last. The campaign is widely credited with helping shape the modern Western tradition of the diamond engagement ring, which was far less common before the middle of the twentieth century.

It helps to remember that the slogan is exactly that, a slogan, and a very good one. It does not set a rule you have to follow, a price you have to hit, or a stone you have to choose. The sentiment, permanence standing in for lasting love, is the durable part, and it applies just as well to a Lab Grown Diamond as to a mined one, because the two are the same material with the same hardness. If you are choosing a ring, the useful takeaway is small and freeing: pick the stone and the design you love, for the person you love. The advertising built the association. The meaning you give the ring is your own.

sources.claim: The slogan "A Diamond Is Forever" was written by copywriter Frances Gerety at the N.W. Ayer agency for De Beers in 1947.
sources.source: Task anchor; N.W. Ayer / De Beers advertising history (Wikipedia, Frances Gerety)

sources.claim: Advertising Age named "A Diamond Is Forever" the number one advertising slogan of the twentieth century.
The De Beers campaign is credited with helping establish the modern Western diamond engagement ring tradition, which was less common before the mid twentieth century.
sources.source: De Beers / N.W. Ayer advertising histories (press and trade accounts)

entries.definition: An annual prong check is a yearly appointment where a jeweler inspects the small metal claws holding your diamond and re-tightens or rebuilds any that have worn down. It is the single best habit for not losing a stone.
entries.body:
Prongs are the small metal tips that curl over the edge of a diamond to hold it in place. They take the brunt of daily life: every time a ring bumps a doorknob, a countertop, or a car door, the prongs absorb it. Over months and years that contact wears the metal thin, and a thin prong can bend, snag, or break. When a prong fails, the stone can loosen and fall out, which is why worn prongs are worth taking seriously. The risk is almost entirely preventable with a regular check.

The fix is a quick inspection about once a year. A jeweler looks at each prong under magnification, checks that the tips still sit firmly over the stone, and feels whether the diamond has any play in the setting. If a prong is worn, they can re-tip it by adding a small amount of metal to the end, or, if several are worn, rebuild the head. You can sometimes catch a warning sign yourself: if you tap the ring gently near your ear and hear a faint rattle, or if the stone visibly shifts, have it looked at sooner.

Many jewelers do this inspection for free, and some jewelry insurance policies expect regular maintenance as a condition of coverage. Rings worn every day, and rings with tall or delicate settings, benefit from a check more often than once a year. Pairing the prong check with a professional cleaning turns it into a single easy annual habit that keeps the stone secure and the ring bright.
related: Cleaning a Diamond Ring at Home

entries.definition: An Asscher cut is a square step cut with cut corners, essentially a square emerald cut with deeper, more pronounced steps that draw the eye toward the center in an X or windmill pattern. It reads as a nearly octagonal square with concentric squares of light.
entries.body:
The Asscher belongs to the step-cut family, the same family as the emerald cut. Its outline is square rather than rectangular, with the four corners cut off so that it appears almost octagonal. The step facets are usually cut steeper and deeper than an emerald cut's, and the pavilion draws down to a small center, producing concentric squares and an X-shaped play of light when you look straight down through the table.

Face-up, an Asscher gives the same restrained, glassy character as an emerald cut rather than bright sparkle. The layered squares create a sense of depth, sometimes called a hall-of-mirrors effect. Being square, it covers the finger differently from an elongated emerald cut, reading as compact and geometric.

As with any step cut, the open, mirror-like facets show the inside of the stone clearly, so inclusions and body color are easier to see than in a brilliant cut. The practical note is the same as for the emerald cut: favor a higher clarity and color grade than you would for a sparkly round, because there is nowhere for a flaw to hide.

On durability the cut corners work in your favor, removing the sharp ninety-degree points that would otherwise be fragile, so an Asscher sets securely. A well-cut Asscher is prized for symmetry: the concentric squares should be perfectly centered, and any tilt is easy to spot when you view the stone face-up.

entries.definition: A bezel setting surrounds the diamond with a continuous metal rim that grips the stone around its girdle. It is the most protective common setting, covering and cushioning the stone's edges.
entries.body:
In a bezel setting, a thin wall of metal is shaped to follow the outline of the stone and then pressed over the edge so it holds the diamond the way a frame holds a picture. A full bezel encircles the whole stone. Because the rim covers the girdle, the thin outer edge where chips usually start, and sits flush or nearly flush with the metal, a bezel is the setting of choice for people who work with their hands, wear the ring constantly, or simply want the least maintenance. Nothing sticks up to snag, and there are no prong tips to wear down or re-tip.

The trade-offs are light and appearance. A rim of metal around the stone blocks light from entering at the edges, so a bezeled diamond can look slightly smaller and a touch less bright than the same stone in prongs, though a well-made bezel keeps this minimal. The metal frame can also make a stone read a shade darker at the perimeter. Bezels suit almost every shape, including round, oval, emerald, and cushion, and a skilled setter can bezel pointed shapes too, which actually protects fragile points well. Bezels come with a high-polish edge or with a milgrain (beaded) edge. Because the rim is structural, a bezeled ring can be a little harder to resize if the design carries detail right up to the setting, so it helps to settle on the size before the stone is set.

entries.definition: The bow-tie effect is a dark, bow-tie-shaped shadow across the center of many elongated diamond shapes. It is normal light behavior in these outlines, not a flaw.
entries.body:
The bow-tie effect is a shadow shaped like a bow tie, or two dark triangles meeting point to point, seen across the middle of many elongated diamonds when you look at them face-up. It shows up in ovals, pears, marquises, and elongated hexagonal cuts, among others.

It's a product of geometry, not a defect. In a stretched outline, some of the pavilion facets across the center send light back at an angle that lines up with the head and body of the person looking down at the stone. Those facets end up reflecting the viewer's own shadow instead of bright light, and the result is a darkened band across the width. Because it comes from the shape itself, essentially every elongated brilliant cut shows some bow-tie. It's expected.

What varies is degree, and that's the useful thing to look at. A well-cut stone shows a faint, even bow-tie that fills in with light and mostly disappears as the diamond moves. A poorly proportioned one shows a heavy, static black band that stays dark and does not brighten with movement. So a light bow-tie is normal and often invisible in everyday wear, while a very dark one is simply worth noting when you compare stones. Round brilliants do not show a bow-tie, because their even symmetry returns light uniformly across the face.

entries.definition: Brilliance is the brightness a diamond returns to the eye: the white light reflected from its surface and, mostly, from inside the stone after it bounces off the pavilion facets. In GIA's cut model this white light return is called brightness, one of the three components of face-up appearance.
entries.body:
Brilliance has two parts. External brilliance is white light that reflects straight off the surface of the crown facets and the table. Internal brilliance is light that enters the stone, bounces off the pavilion facets by total internal reflection, and returns up through the crown and table to your eye. Most of the brightness you see in a well-cut diamond is internal.

Whether that internal light comes back depends on cut proportions, not on carat, color, or clarity. If the pavilion is cut too shallow or too deep, light meets the facets at the wrong angle, passes through, and leaks out the bottom instead of returning. That lost light reads as dull or dark areas. A diamond cut to good angles keeps light bouncing until it exits back toward the viewer, which is why cut is the C that governs brilliance.

GIA's cut grading system, released in January 2006 for round brilliants, treats face-up appearance as three effects: brightness (the white light return that people loosely call brilliance), fire, and scintillation. GIA grades round brilliant cut from Excellent to Poor.

Brightness is a property of the diamond material and the cut, not of origin. A Lab Grown Diamond is the same carbon crystal as a mined one, with the same refractive index, so a well-cut Lab Grown Diamond returns light the same way a well-cut mined diamond does. Brilliance is also separate from fire: brilliance is the white light, fire is the colored flashes. The two shift a little with cut, and a balanced cut aims to deliver both. To judge brilliance yourself, look at the stone face-up in mixed lighting and watch how evenly it lights up across the whole table.

sources.claim: GIA released its round brilliant cut grading system in January 2006; it assesses face-up appearance through brightness (white light return), fire, and scintillation, and assigns a cut grade from Excellent to Poor.
sources.source: GIA (Gemological Institute of America), Diamond Cut grading system, gia.edu / 4cs.gia.edu

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically the same material as a mined diamond, so it shares the same optical properties.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: A carat is the standard unit of weight for a diamond, equal to exactly 200 milligrams (0.2 grams). It measures weight, not physical size or diameter, and each carat divides into 100 points.
entries.body:
Carat is usually the first C a buyer asks about, because it's the one figure that reads like a headline number. It rewards precise understanding. One metric carat equals exactly 200 milligrams, and jewelers split each carat into 100 points, so a 25-point diamond weighs a quarter of a carat. Don't confuse carat with karat: carat is the weight of a gemstone, while karat measures the purity of gold. They sound identical and mean different things.

The most useful thing to know about carat is that weight isn't the same as face-up size. Two diamonds of equal carat weight can present very different diameters across the top, depending on how each one is cut. A stone cut too deep carries weight in its lower body, below the girdle, where the eye never sees it. A well-proportioned stone spreads more of its weight into the visible face and can look larger than its weight alone suggests. For that reason carat should be read together with cut, never in isolation, and millimeter measurements often tell you more about apparent size than the carat figure by itself.

Carat weight says nothing about a diamond's origin or its standing on the other three C's. Lab Grown Diamonds are weighed on the identical carat scale, and a carat is a carat regardless of how the crystal formed. For a sense of the upper limit, the Cullinan, the largest gem-quality rough diamond ever found, weighed 3,106 carats.

sources.claim: The Cullinan is the largest gem-quality rough diamond ever found, at 3,106 carats, recovered in 1905.

entries.definition: A cathedral setting supports the center diamond with two arches of metal that sweep up from the band to the stone, like the arches of a cathedral. The arches add height, security, and a graceful profile.
entries.body:
In a cathedral setting, the shoulders of the band do not run straight into the center stone. Instead they rise in curved arches that meet the setting on each side, cradling the stone from below and lifting it up. The name comes from the resemblance to the arched, vaulted architecture of a cathedral. Those arches are structural: they brace the center setting and can make a ring feel more substantial without adding much weight or cost, since they are metal rather than extra diamonds.

The cathedral style raises the center stone higher off the finger, which shows it off and makes it easy to see from the side, and the arches give a designer a natural place to add pave, milgrain, or other detail. Height is the main trade-off to weigh. A taller setting catches the eye but also catches on things more easily and sits up where it can take knocks, so if you want a low, snag-free profile, a lower cathedral or a different setting may suit you better. The arches can also collect a little more lotion and dust underneath, so an occasional clean keeps the stone bright. A cathedral works with almost any center shape and pairs equally well with a plain solitaire band or a diamond-set one. It is a way to add drama and support through metalwork rather than through additional stones, which keeps the focus on the center diamond.

entries.definition: Certification labs, more precisely gemological grading laboratories, are independent organizations that examine and grade diamonds to published standards. For Lab Grown Diamonds today the main names are GIA, IGI, and GCAL, and the landscape shifted noticeably in 2025 and 2026.
entries.body:
GIA, the Gemological Institute of America, is the nonprofit founded by Robert M. Shipley in 1931 that built the 4Cs framework the whole trade uses. It grades both mined and laboratory-grown stones. On October 1, 2025, GIA moved its Lab Grown Diamond reports to a Premium and Standard tier structure, a change from the letter-and-number 4C grades it long issued for them.

IGI, the International Gemological Institute, was founded in Antwerp in 1975 and is the largest grading laboratory by volume of reports. It's the dominant lab for Lab Grown Diamonds, and it still issues full 4C reports for them, stating color, clarity, cut, and carat the traditional way. For that reason most laboratory-grown stones on the market today carry an IGI report.

GCAL, the Gem Certification and Assurance Lab, was founded in New York in 2001 and is known for guaranteed grading and its 8X cut grade for round brilliants, introduced in 2021. It grades laboratory-grown as well as mined diamonds and is a respected independent option, particularly for buyers who value its cut analysis.

One more shift marks the current landscape: HRD Antwerp, a long-standing European lab, stopped grading loose Lab Grown Diamonds in 2026. The practical takeaway is straightforward. Match the diamond to its report by the laser-inscribed number, read the grades on their own terms, and remember that a laboratory-grown diamond is a real diamond that these same laboratories grade by the same science. Which lab graded a stone tells you the standard behind the paperwork, which is simply useful to know.

sources.claim: GIA (Gemological Institute of America) is a nonprofit founded by Robert M. Shipley in 1931.
GIA moved its Lab Grown Diamond reports to Premium and Standard tiers on October 1, 2025.
IGI (International Gemological Institute) was founded in Antwerp in 1975.
IGI is the largest grading laboratory by report volume and still issues full 4C reports for Lab Grown Diamonds.
GCAL (Gem Certification and Assurance Lab) was founded in New York in 2001.
sources.source: Gem Certification and Assurance Lab (GCAL) / American Gem Society

sources.claim: GCAL introduced its 8X cut grade for round brilliants in 2021.
HRD Antwerp stopped grading loose Lab Grown Diamonds in 2026.
Laboratory-grown diamonds are real diamonds under the FTC Jewelry Guides.
sources.source: US Federal Trade Commission, Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: A clarity grade rates how free a diamond is of internal inclusions and surface blemishes when examined under 10x magnification, on a scale from Flawless down to Included. It describes tiny features inside and on the stone, most of which the naked eye can't see.
entries.body:
The GIA scale, from best to most included, runs Flawless (FL), Internally Flawless (IF), Very Very Slightly Included (VVS1 and VVS2), Very Slightly Included (VS1 and VS2), Slightly Included (SI1 and SI2), and Included (I1, I2, I3). The reference condition is always 10x magnification: a Flawless diamond shows no inclusions or blemishes to a skilled grader at 10x, while an SI or I stone has features that are easier to find.

Internal features are called inclusions and surface features are called blemishes. They take many forms, such as tiny crystals, feathers, clouds, and needles, and a grade reflects their size, number, position, nature, and how much they affect the stone. Two diamonds with the same grade can look different, because an inclusion sitting under the table where you look straight in matters more than one tucked near the girdle under a prong.

For most buyers the practical target is a diamond that's clean to the unaided eye, which the trade calls eye-clean. Plenty of VS and even SI1 stones qualify, so a grade a step or two below Flawless often looks identical to the eye, with the difference living only under the loupe.

Lab Grown Diamonds are graded on the same clarity scale and assessed the same way under 10x. Their internal characteristics can differ in type from those in mined diamonds, but the grading language and the magnification standard are identical, and IGI still issues full 4C reports stating clarity.

sources.claim: IGI still issues full 4C grading reports, including a clarity grade, for Lab Grown Diamonds.

entries.term: Cleaning a Diamond Ring at Home
entries.definition: You can clean a diamond ring at home with warm water, a little mild dish soap, and a soft brush. Soak it a few minutes, gently brush around and behind the stone, rinse, and pat dry.
entries.body:
A diamond ring picks up a film of hand lotion, soap scum, cooking oils, and skin oil over ordinary wear. Diamond is oleophilic, meaning grease clings to it, so even a spotless-looking stone dulls a little as that film builds. The good news is that removing it is simple and safe to do yourself.

Fill a small bowl with warm (not boiling) water and add a few drops of mild dish soap. Let the ring soak for ten to fifteen minutes to loosen the buildup. Then use a soft toothbrush to gently work around the stone, along the setting, and especially underneath the diamond, where the most grime collects and where light enters the pavilion. Rinse under warm running water, with a stopper in the drain or the ring held in a strainer so it can't slip away, and dry with a soft lint-free cloth.

A gentle cleaning every week or two keeps a ring bright. Diamond itself is the hardest natural material, a 10 on the Mohs scale, so the stone is not at risk from a soft brush. Skip the harsh stuff: chlorine bleach and abrasive cleansers can pit and discolor the gold or platinum alloys that hold the stone, and toothpaste is abrasive enough to be hard on the metal too. Warm water and mild soap are all you need. The same routine works for any diamond ring, mined or Lab Grown, since the material and the settings are the same.

sources.claim: Diamond ranks 10, the maximum, on the Mohs scale of mineral hardness.
sources.source: Mohs scale of mineral hardness (Friedrich Mohs)

entries.definition: A color grade rates how close a diamond is to having no color at all, on GIA's D-to-Z scale where D is completely colorless and Z shows a light yellow or brown tint. In this range less color is rarer, and the grade describes the faint body color you're trying not to see.
entries.body:
The standard scale runs from D at the colorless top to Z at the light end, divided into named bands: D to F is colorless, G to J is near colorless, K to M is faint, N to R is very light, and S to Z is light. Graders judge color by comparing a stone, table-down, against a set of masterstones under controlled lighting, so the grade reflects a diamond's body color rather than any flash of rainbow you see in normal wear.

The scale famously begins at D rather than A. When GIA built its modern system it deliberately skipped the earlier letters, because older trade grading had used A, B, and C loosely and inconsistently, and starting fresh at D avoided inheriting that confusion. Richard Liddicoat's grading scales, introduced in 1953, set this framework that the trade still uses today.

Most differences of a single grade are invisible once a diamond is set, especially across the D-to-J range, and they get harder to see in warmer metals like yellow or rose gold. This is why many buyers choose a near-colorless grade that faces up white to the eye rather than a top grade whose advantage only a grader beside a masterstone would notice.

Diamonds with obvious, saturated color fall outside this scale entirely and are graded as fancy-color stones on a separate system. Lab Grown Diamonds are color-graded on the same D-to-Z scale, and IGI still issues full 4C reports that state the color grade for them.

sources.claim: GIA's D-to-Z color scale was introduced as part of Richard Liddicoat's International Diamond Grading System in 1953, and the scale starts at D to avoid confusion with earlier trade systems that had used A, B and C.
IGI still issues full 4C grading reports, including a color grade, for Lab Grown Diamonds.

entries.definition: The crown is the upper part of a diamond, the whole section above the girdle. It includes the flat table and the angled facets that ring it.
entries.body:
The crown is everything above the girdle. It holds the flat table at the top and the ring of angled facets around it, which on a round brilliant are the star facets, the bezel (kite) facets, and the upper girdle facets.

The crown has two jobs. It lets light into the stone, and as light leaves, its angled facets bend and split white light into spectral colors, which is the source of a diamond's fire. The steepness of these facets is called the crown angle, measured against the flat plane of the girdle. Crown angle doesn't act alone: it works with the pavilion angle underneath to set the balance between brightness and fire. A crown cut too shallow or too steep pushes that balance off, and the stone can lose either sparkle or color.

Crown height, given as a percentage of the stone's width, is one of the proportions a grading lab records. Read on its own it means little; read together with table size, pavilion depth, and the two key angles, it helps describe how well a stone is cut. In short, the crown is the part of the diamond you look into and the part that makes colored flashes, while the pavilion below is the part that sends light back to your eye.

entries.definition: Cubic zirconia (CZ) is a lab-made crystalline form of zirconium dioxide (ZrO2), stabilized into a cubic structure and cut as an inexpensive, colorless diamond simulant. It is a different material from diamond, softer and denser, and is not a type of diamond.
entries.body:
Zirconium dioxide does not naturally hold a cubic crystal form at room temperature, so cubic zirconia has to be coaxed into one. Makers add a stabilizer such as yttrium oxide or calcium oxide and melt the mixture at extreme temperature (zirconia melts near 2,750 degrees Celsius) using a skull-melting method, in which the outer shell of the charge stays solid and acts as its own crucible. The result is a hard, clear, flawless crystal that can be cut to look convincingly like diamond.

The technique was developed by Soviet scientists at the Lebedev Physical Institute in Moscow, with the method published in 1973. Gem-quality cubic zirconia reached the jewelry market in 1977, when the Ceres Corporation began commercial production in the United States. It became one of the most widely used diamond simulants, and it remains common today.

Compared with diamond, CZ is softer, about 8 to 8.5 on the Mohs scale versus 10, so it scratches and abrades more easily over years of wear. It is much denser, so a CZ weighs noticeably more than a diamond of the same physical size. Its dispersion, or fire, is higher than diamond's, around 0.058 to 0.066, which can give it an extra-colorful flash that experienced eyes read as not diamond. It is also a poor conductor of heat, so a thermal diamond tester will not mistake it for diamond, unlike moissanite. Cubic zirconia is a legitimate, affordable stone. It simply is not diamond, and reputable sellers describe it plainly as what it is.

sources.claim: The skull-melting process for producing cubic zirconia crystals was developed by Soviet researchers at the Lebedev Physical Institute in Moscow, with the method published in 1973.
sources.source: Lebedev Physical Institute (V. V. Osiko group); cubic zirconia history, per encyclopedic and gemological references

sources.claim: Gem-quality cubic zirconia reached the jewelry market when the Ceres Corporation began commercial production in the United States, using crystals stabilized with yttria.
sources.source: Cubic zirconia commercial production history, per encyclopedic and gemological references

entries.definition: The culet is the point or small facet at the very bottom of a diamond, where the pavilion facets meet. On many modern stones it is a clean point, graded as none.
entries.body:
The culet is the point or tiny facet at the very bottom of a diamond, where the pavilion facets come together. On many modern stones there's no facet there at all, just a clean point, which a grading report lists as a culet of none or pointed.

Historically, cutters polished a small facet across the bottom tip to guard the fragile point against chipping, and older styles such as the old mine cut and the old European cut almost always show a visible culet. Improvements in cutting and in setting mean today's round brilliants are often finished to a point instead.

When a culet facet is present, it is graded by size, on a scale that runs from very small up to extremely large. A large culet is a problem you can see: looking straight down through the table, it appears as a small open window or a gray dot in the center of the stone, which can be distracting. For that reason a small culet or none at all is generally preferred now. A pointed culet is the single most vulnerable point on a diamond to a hard, direct knock, which is exactly why cutters used to face it over; in a well-set ring worn normally that risk is small, but it is not zero. This is also the facet that explains the round brilliant's two facet counts: a pointed stone has fifty-seven facets, and one with a culet facet has fifty-eight.

entries.definition: A cushion cut is a square-to-rectangular diamond with rounded corners and gently curved sides, faceted in the brilliant style, so that it resembles a pillow. It offers soft, rounded sparkle and a slightly antique character.
entries.body:
The cushion is a modified brilliant whose outline is a square or rectangle with the corners and sides rounded, giving it the pillow shape that names it. Brilliant-style faceting fills the interior, and cushions come in two broad faceting styles: a chunky pattern with larger, bolder facets, and a crushed-ice pattern with many smaller facets that scatter light into a glittery, granular sparkle.

Face-up, a cushion is softer and rounder than a princess and warmer in character than a round. It handles light well and is available in square and elongated versions, the latter flattering the finger the way any stretched shape does. Because the corners are rounded rather than pointed, a cushion has no fragile tips and sets securely, which makes it one of the more durable fancy shapes.

The honest practical note is that cushion covers a wide range of looks. A chunky cushion and a crushed-ice cushion can both be sold under the same name while looking quite different in the hand, one with distinct flashes and the other with a shimmering, less defined sparkle. Neither is better; it is a matter of taste. So it is worth asking which faceting style a given cushion uses, and viewing it face-up, rather than assuming all cushions sparkle alike. Length-to-width ratio also varies, from a true square near 1.0 to a clearly elongated rectangle, so the same name can describe very different proportions.

entries.definition: A cut grade rates how well a diamond's proportions, symmetry, and polish return light to the eye, which is what makes a diamond look bright and lively. GIA grades cut for standard round brilliant diamonds on a five-step scale from Excellent to Poor.
entries.body:
Of the four C's, cut is the one most under human control and the one that most drives how a diamond actually looks. Color and clarity describe the raw crystal; cut describes the craft applied to it. A cutter chooses angles and proportions that either send light back up through the top of the stone or leak it out the bottom and sides. Get the geometry right and even a modest color or clarity grade can look stunning; get it wrong and a high-color, high-clarity stone can look dull and lifeless.

The science behind modern proportions goes back to Marcel Tolkowsky, whose 1919 analysis described the angle relationships that make a round brilliant perform well. The round brilliant has 57 or 58 facets (the 58th being a tiny flat facet at the bottom called the culet), an arrangement engineered to maximize returned light.

GIA released its cut grading system for round brilliants in 2006, assigning one of five grades: Excellent, Very Good, Good, Fair, or Poor. The grade weighs brightness, fire, and scintillation together with the stone's proportions, polish, and symmetry.

That five-step grade applies only to round brilliants. Fancy shapes such as ovals, emeralds, and the elongated hexagonal Dutch Marquise (a trade name that reads "Hexagonal Modified Brilliant" on an IGI report) don't receive a GIA cut grade, so with those you judge cut by proportions, symmetry, and how the stone looks to your eye. Lab Grown Diamonds are cut and graded by the very same standards.

sources.claim: Marcel Tolkowsky's 1919 analysis (Diamond Design) described the proportion relationships underlying the modern round brilliant cut, which has 57 or 58 facets.
GIA released its cut grading system for standard round brilliant diamonds in 2006, with five grades from Excellent to Poor.
sources.source: Gemological Institute of America (GIA), reported by JCK

sources.claim: The Dutch Marquise is a trade name for an elongated hexagonal cut and is described as "Hexagonal Modified Brilliant" on an IGI report.

entries.definition: CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) is a method of growing diamond by breaking a carbon-rich gas down inside a vacuum chamber so that carbon atoms settle onto a thin diamond seed and build a crystal up layer by layer.
entries.body:
CVD grows a diamond from gas. The process starts with a thin, flat slice of diamond called a seed, placed inside a sealed vacuum chamber. A carbon-rich gas, usually methane, is fed in along with hydrogen. Energy, typically microwaves, heats the gas into a glowing plasma and breaks the molecules apart, freeing individual carbon atoms. Those atoms settle onto the seed and bond to it, and the crystal thickens one atomic layer at a time. Because the growth happens at relatively low pressure, CVD is sometimes described as low-pressure synthesis, in contrast to the high-pressure HPHT method.

CVD gives growers fine control over the conditions in the chamber, which is one reason the method is widely used for gem-quality material. It tends to produce diamonds low in nitrogen, a category known as Type IIa that is uncommon among mined diamonds. The crystal grows as a squared-off tabular block rather than the shape a mined diamond takes, and it's later cut and polished like any other rough.

Fresh out of the chamber, CVD diamonds sometimes carry a faint brown or gray tint from the way the crystal grew. Growers often follow with post-growth treatment (HPHT annealing) to move the color toward colorless. That step is standard, permanent, and disclosed on the grading report. The finished stone is a genuine diamond, the same carbon and the same hardness as a mined one; CVD is simply the method that produced it.

entries.definition: Depth percentage is a diamond's total height (table to culet) divided by its average girdle diameter, expressed as a percentage. It tells you how deep or shallow the stone is cut relative to its width.
entries.body:
Depth percentage is a single number that tells you how tall a diamond is compared with how wide it is. The formula is the total depth, measured from the table on top down to the culet at the bottom, divided by the average girdle diameter, then multiplied by one hundred.

It matters because depth controls how light travels through the stone. Cut too shallow, and light slips out through the bottom rather than reflecting back up, so the stone can look watery, glassy, or washed out. Cut too deep, and light escapes out the sides instead of the top, the center can go dark, and weight ends up buried in the height where it does not add visible spread, so the stone can face up smaller than its carat weight suggests. A depth in the favorable range keeps light bouncing back toward your eye.

What counts as favorable depends on the shape and on the grading lab's cut standards, and round brilliants are held to a tighter window than fancy shapes. Depth percentage is never read alone; it's weighed together with table percentage and the crown and pavilion angles, since one number cannot describe a whole cut. Marcel Tolkowsky's 1919 proportions were an early published attempt to pin down the depth and angles that make a round brilliant bright.

sources.claim: Marcel Tolkowsky published influential round brilliant proportions, covering depth and facet angles, in 1919.

entries.definition: Diamond is a crystalline form of pure carbon, with each carbon atom bonded to four others in a rigid cubic (isometric) lattice. That structure makes it the hardest known natural material, a 10 on the Mohs scale, and an exceptional conductor of heat.
entries.body:
Diamond is carbon, the same element found in graphite and charcoal, but arranged very differently. In graphite the carbon atoms sit in loose sheets that slide past one another, which is why it feels slippery and marks paper. In diamond every carbon atom is locked to four neighbors in a three-dimensional tetrahedral framework, and that dense, uniform bonding is the source of nearly every property diamond is prized for.

Optically, diamond has a high refractive index of about 2.42 and strong dispersion, the splitting of white light into spectral colors, of about 0.044. A well-cut stone returns a lot of light to the eye, which reads as brightness and fire. Diamond is singly refractive, so it does not double the appearance of the facets seen through it.

In nature, diamond forms deep in the Earth's mantle under high pressure and temperature and is carried toward the surface in volcanic rock. It can also be grown in a laboratory. A Lab Grown Diamond is the same material, with the same crystal structure and the same properties. Under U.S. rules a diamond is defined by its composition, not its origin, so a Lab Grown Diamond is a diamond, not an imitation.

Diamond's hardness, a 10 on the Mohs scale, means only another diamond will readily scratch it. It is also the best thermal conductor of any gemstone, which is the principle behind diamond testers. Hardness is not the same as toughness, though. Diamond has perfect cleavage in four directions, so a sharp blow in the wrong spot can still chip it.

sources.claim: The FTC defines a diamond as a mineral consisting essentially of pure carbon crystallized in the isometric system, a definition covering both mined diamonds and Lab Grown Diamonds (the qualifier 'natural' was removed).
sources.source: FTC, Guides for the Jewelry, Precious Metals, and Pewter Industries (Federal Register)

sources.claim: Diamond sits at 10, the top of the Mohs hardness scale.
sources.source: Mohs hardness scale (Friedrich Mohs), per Encyclopaedia Britannica

entries.definition: Mostly no. Almost all natural diamonds form deep in Earth's mantle from carbon-rich material, and most are far older than the land plants that coal is made from, so coal is not their source.
entries.body:
Mostly no. The popular image of a lump of coal squeezed into a diamond makes a nice story, but it does not match the geology. Almost all natural diamonds form deep inside the Earth's mantle, roughly 90 miles or more below the surface, where extreme pressure and heat let carbon crystallize over very long spans of time. They are carried up toward the surface much later by rare, deep volcanic eruptions that leave behind a rock called kimberlite.

Coal is a different material made in a different place. It forms near the surface from the remains of plants, under far lower pressure and temperature. The timelines do not line up either. Most natural diamonds are more than a billion years old, hundreds of millions of years before the first land plants existed. Since coal is made of plant matter, it simply was not around when most diamonds were forming. The carbon in diamonds comes instead from carbon-bearing minerals and fluids deep in the mantle.

One nuance, for completeness: a rare few diamonds hold carbon that was once near the surface and got pulled deep by plate tectonics. That still is not coal. And Lab Grown Diamonds skip geology altogether, grown in machines from a carbon source, yet they are the same material, real diamonds, as the FTC confirmed in its 2018 jewelry guidance.

sources.claim: Most natural diamonds form deep in Earth's mantle, roughly 150 kilometers (about 90 miles) or more below the surface, under high pressure and temperature, and are carried upward by kimberlite volcanic eruptions.
sources.source: GIA, "Diamonds from the Deep: How Do Diamonds Form in the Deep Earth?" (Gems & Gemology, Winter 2018); geology.com

sources.claim: Most natural diamonds are more than a billion years old, hundreds of millions of years older than Earth's first land plants (the source material for coal), so coal is not their carbon source.
sources.source: GIA, "Diamonds from the Deep: How Old Are Diamonds? Are They Forever?" (Gems & Gemology, Spring 2019); geology.com

sources.claim: A rare minority of diamonds contain carbon that originated near the surface and was carried deep by plate tectonics; this is still not coal.
sources.source: GIA, "Diamonds from the Deep" series (Gems & Gemology, 2018 to 2019), on deep-carbon recycling via subduction

sources.claim: Lab Grown Diamonds are grown from a carbon source rather than mined and are real diamonds.

entries.term: Do Lab Grown Diamonds Cloud or Fade?
entries.definition: No. Lab Grown Diamonds do not cloud, fade, yellow, or degrade over time; a Lab Grown Diamond is crystallized carbon, and its color and clarity are fixed properties of the crystal that don't change with age or wear.
entries.body:
This is one of the most common worries about Lab Grown Diamonds, and the answer is a plain no. A diamond, grown or mined, is carbon locked in an extremely stable crystal structure and rated 10 on the Mohs scale, the hardest of any natural material. Its color and clarity are set by the arrangement of the crystal itself, not by any coating or dye, so they don't drift over time. Sunlight, water, heat from daily life, and normal wear don't cloud it, fade it, or turn it yellow.

The worry usually comes from mixing up Lab Grown Diamonds with diamond simulants. Cubic zirconia, a different and softer material, can abrade and grow cloudy after years of wear. That's a real thing that happens, but it happens to cubic zirconia, not to diamond. Because a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, it behaves like one, staying clear and bright.

Color treatment doesn't change this either. When a Lab Grown Diamond has had its color improved by HPHT annealing, that change is permanent and stable, so a treated stone doesn't revert under everyday conditions.

There is one ordinary exception, and it isn't the stone changing. Any diamond can look dull when a film of skin oil, soap, or lotion builds up on it. That's surface grime sitting on top of the diamond, not the diamond degrading, and a quick clean with mild soap and water brings the sparkle back. The crystal underneath is exactly as it was the day it was set.

entries.definition: A Dutch Marquise is an elongated hexagonal cut diamond. It has pointed ends and straight, angular sides, and it is a trade name, not an official gemological shape category; on a laboratory report the same shape is described as a Hexagonal Modified Brilliant.
entries.body:
A Dutch Marquise is an elongated hexagon in outline: two pointed ends joined by straight, angular sides, rather than the curved sides of a true marquise. That straight-sided geometry is what separates it from the classic marquise, or navette, which is a boat shape with two points but sides that curve outward. The Dutch Marquise does not have curved sides and does not have softened or rounded points; its silhouette is angular and hexagonal.

Because Dutch Marquise is a trade name rather than an official gemological category, it is not the wording that appears on a grading report. On an IGI report, the same stone is described by its standard shape classification, Hexagonal Modified Brilliant. The length-to-width ratio of a Dutch Marquise is typically in the range of roughly 1.5 to 2.0 to one, which gives it an elongated look while keeping the clearly hexagonal outline.

Face-up, like other elongated shapes it covers more of the finger than a compact shape of similar weight and lengthens the look of the hand. As with any cut that comes to points, the two ends are the thinnest and most exposed areas, so they benefit from protective prongs or a setting that shields the tips.

One practical note: because it is a trade name, different sellers may apply the term a little differently, so it is worth confirming the actual outline and, on the report, the shape classification and length-to-width ratio, so you know exactly what you are getting.

sources.claim: IGI report LG799689559, issued 2026-05-09 for a diamond cut and sold as a Dutch Marquise, classifies the shape as Hexagonal Modified Brilliant.
sources.source: IGI (International Gemological Institute) diamond grading report No. LG799689559

entries.definition: An east-west setting turns an elongated diamond sideways so its long axis runs across the finger, from side to side, instead of up and down the finger. It is a modern, less common way to set shapes like ovals, marquises, emeralds, and pears.
entries.body:
Most rings set an elongated stone lengthwise, with its long axis running along the finger toward the wrist. An east-west setting rotates that same stone a quarter turn so it lies horizontally, spanning the width of the finger. The stone and the setting are otherwise the same; only the orientation changes. It is a simple choice that gives a familiar shape a distinctly modern, relaxed look.

East-west works best with shapes that have a clear long axis: ovals, marquises, emerald cuts, pears, and other elongated cuts, including the Dutch Marquise, a trade name for an elongated hexagonal cut diamond with pointed ends and straight angular sides. Set sideways, an elongated stone can look wider on the hand and can flatter the finger differently than a lengthwise setting. A few practical points are worth knowing. A horizontal stone sits wider across the finger, so depending on the design it can sit closer to neighboring rings or catch a little more, and pointed shapes still need V-tips on their points no matter the orientation. Because the look is less traditional, it reads as a deliberate style choice, which is part of its appeal for people who want something recognizable but not standard. East-west pairs well with bezels and half bezels, which brace the long sides cleanly, and with simple solitaire bands that let the sideways orientation be the statement. It is purely an aesthetic decision, so it comes down to how the stone looks and feels on your hand.

entries.definition: An elongated cushion is a cushion cut with a rectangular rather than square outline, stretched so its length clearly exceeds its width while keeping the soft rounded corners and pillow silhouette. It is a longer, more finger-flattering version of the standard cushion.
entries.body:
An elongated cushion is not a separate faceting family; it is a cushion cut, a modified brilliant with rounded corners and curved sides, drawn out to a longer length-to-width ratio. Where a classic cushion is close to square, an elongated cushion typically runs at a ratio of roughly 1.2 to one or more, giving it a rectangular pillow shape.

Face-up, that extra length does two useful things. It makes the stone read larger for its carat weight, because the outline spreads the same weight over a longer surface, and it lengthens and slims the look of the finger, the same reason people choose ovals and elongated radiants. The faceting can be the chunky style or the crushed-ice style, exactly as with a square cushion, so the sparkle character depends on the individual stone.

On durability the elongated cushion keeps the standard cushion's advantage: its corners are rounded, not pointed, so there is no fragile tip to chip, and it sits securely in a setting.

The practical point is that length is a preference, not a grade, and there is no single correct ratio. Some people want just a hint of stretch, others a long, narrow rectangle, and two stones of the same carat weight can look quite different depending on how far each is elongated. It is worth looking at the actual length-to-width ratio, and ideally the stone in person, since the number alone does not tell you how it will feel on the hand.

entries.definition: An emerald cut is a rectangular diamond with cut (beveled) corners and step-cut faceting: long, flat, parallel facets stacked in rows like stairs, viewed through a large open table. It trades sparkle for a clean, glassy look and long flashes of light.
entries.body:
The emerald cut is the classic step cut. Instead of the many small triangular facets of a brilliant, it has a smaller number of long rectangular facets running parallel to the girdle, stacked in steps on the crown and pavilion. The corners are cut off at an angle, turning the rectangle into an elongated octagon, and the table, the flat top facet, is large and open.

Face-up, an emerald cut does not sparkle the way a brilliant does. It produces broad, mirror-like flashes and a sense of depth, sometimes described as a hall-of-mirrors effect. Elongated outlines flatter the finger, and the shape reads elegant and understated rather than fiery.

Two honest consequences follow from that open, step-cut design. First, because the table is large and the facets are few and flat, you can see straight into the stone, so inclusions and body color are more visible than in a busy brilliant cut that masks them. Second, the same openness makes any lack of transparency obvious.

On durability the emerald cut is well behaved: its beveled corners remove the sharp points that make other fancy shapes fragile, so it sets securely. The practical takeaway is to favor a slightly higher clarity and color grade than you might accept in a round, and to view the stone face-up, since a step cut hides nothing. This is not about second-guessing anyone; it is simply how a transparent, open cut works.

entries.definition: Eye-clean describes a diamond with no inclusions or blemishes visible to a person with normal vision at a typical viewing distance. It's a practical trade description, not an official grade on the clarity scale.
entries.body:
Clarity grades are assigned under 10x magnification, but you don't wear a diamond at 10x. Eye-clean bridges that gap by describing what the stone looks like to the unaided eye at normal viewing distance, usually taken to be roughly a foot away, in ordinary light. A diamond can carry inclusions that a grader records under the loupe yet show none of them to the naked eye once it's set and on a hand.

This matters because eye-clean stones appear across a wide span of clarity grades. Flawless and Internally Flawless diamonds are eye-clean by definition, and so are essentially all VVS and VS stones. Many SI1 diamonds are eye-clean too, and even some SI2 stones are, depending on where their inclusions sit and how large they are. A diamond several steps down the clarity scale can therefore look identical, to your eye, to one near the top. That's the whole value of the idea: you can choose clarity by what you can actually see rather than by a number read under a loupe.

Eye-clean isn't printed on a grading report, because it depends on the observer, the distance, and the individual stone rather than a fixed laboratory standard. The reliable way to judge it is to look at the specific diamond, or a clear image of it, and check whether anything catches your eye. Larger stones and step-cut shapes like emerald cuts show inclusions more readily, so the eye-clean question deserves more attention as size goes up. Lab Grown Diamonds are assessed for eye-cleanliness in exactly the same way.

entries.definition: A facet is a single flat, polished surface on a diamond. The number, shape, angle, and alignment of all the facets together make up the cut.
entries.body:
A facet is one flat, polished plane on the surface of a diamond. Each one behaves either as a window, letting light pass in or out, or as a mirror, bouncing light around inside the stone. Taken together, the number of facets, their shapes, their angles, and how precisely they line up make up what we call the cut, and the cut is what produces brightness, fire (flashes of color), and scintillation (the sparkle you see as the stone moves).

The modern round brilliant is the reference design. It has fifty-seven facets, or fifty-eight if a small culet facet is included, arranged as thirty-three on the crown and twenty-four on the pavilion. Marcel Tolkowsky published an influential set of proportions for this cut in 1919. Other shapes use other schemes: step cuts such as the emerald and Asscher use long, parallel facets set like stairsteps for a clean hall-of-mirrors look, while brilliant-style cuts use triangular and kite-shaped facets for maximum sparkle.

Facet edges should meet cleanly at sharp points; extra, missing, or misaligned facets are symmetry faults. These structural terms apply the same way to Lab Grown Diamonds and mined diamonds, because the two are the same material, cut on the same wheels, to the same geometry. A facet is simply where light gets its job done.

sources.claim: Marcel Tolkowsky published an influential set of proportions for the round brilliant cut in 1919.
Lab Grown Diamonds are real diamonds, the same material as mined diamonds.

entries.definition: Finding your ring size means measuring the finger the ring will sit on to get a size number. The most reliable way is to have a jeweler size you with a set of metal ring gauges, but you can also measure at home with a ring sizer or by measuring a ring that already fits.
entries.body:
Ring size is a standardized measurement of the inside of a band. In the United States it is expressed as a number, with half sizes, that corresponds to a specific inside diameter and circumference. Getting it right matters, because a ring that is too loose can spin or slip off, and one that is too tight is uncomfortable and hard to remove.

The most accurate method is professional sizing. A jeweler slips a set of graduated metal rings, called a ring gauge, onto your finger until one fits, giving a true size on the finger you will actually wear the ring on. If you cannot get to a jeweler, there are good at-home options. Plastic or paper ring sizers work the same way as the gauge. You can also measure a ring that already fits that finger by measuring the inside diameter in millimeters across the center and matching it to a size chart. A strip of paper or string wrapped around the finger, marked, and measured against a chart works too, though it is the least precise.

A few things throw sizing off. Fingers swell over the day and in heat, so measure when your hands are warm and normal-sized, ideally later in the day rather than first thing in the morning or when you are cold. Account for the knuckle: the ring has to slide over it, so if your knuckle is larger than the base of the finger, size to clear the knuckle. Wide bands fit more snugly than thin ones, so size up slightly for a wide band. When in doubt, most rings can be resized later, though full eternity bands and certain settings are difficult or impossible to resize.

entries.definition: Fire is the flashes of spectral color a diamond throws when white light splits into its component colors inside the stone. Its physical cause is dispersion: a diamond's refractive index is slightly different for each wavelength, so each color bends by a different amount.
entries.body:
White light is a mix of all colors. When it enters a diamond it slows and bends, and because the diamond bends violet light a little more than red light, the colors fan out and leave the stone along slightly different paths. Your eye catches those separated colors as brief red, orange, blue, and violet flashes. That is fire.

The amount of spreading is set by a material constant called dispersion, the difference between the refractive index at a red wavelength and at a violet one. For diamond that value is about 0.044, high enough for strong, saturated flashes but not so high that the stone looks gaudy. Some imitations disperse much more: cubic zirconia is near 0.060 and moissanite near 0.104, which is why they can throw exaggerated rainbow flashes that a trained eye uses to tell them apart from diamond.

Fire depends on cut as much as on material. Facets act as tiny prisms, and their angles decide whether the separated colors travel far enough apart to be seen before they exit. Steeper crown angles and smaller tables tend to favor fire, while very flat crowns favor white brightness, so a good cut balances the two. Fire also shows best under a single bright point source, like a spotlight or the sun, rather than flat, even lighting: a diamond can look subdued under diffuse office light and show much more color under a spotlight or in sunlight.

GIA's cut grading system counts fire as one of the three parts of a round brilliant's face-up appearance, alongside brightness and scintillation. Origin does not change dispersion, so a Lab Grown Diamond has the same 0.044 value and the same fire as a mined diamond of equal cut.

sources.claim: GIA's round brilliant cut grading system (released January 2006) assesses face-up appearance through brightness, fire, and scintillation.
sources.source: GIA (Gemological Institute of America), Diamond Cut grading system, gia.edu / 4cs.gia.edu

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically the same material as a mined diamond, so it has the same dispersion.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: Fluorescence is the visible glow, most often blue, that some diamonds give off under ultraviolet light such as sunlight or a black light. Labs rate its strength from None to Very Strong, and for most diamonds it has little or no effect on how the stone looks in ordinary light.
entries.body:
Roughly speaking, fluorescence happens because certain trace elements and structural features in a diamond absorb ultraviolet energy and re-emit it as visible light. The common color is blue, though other colors occur. GIA reports fluorescence on a scale of None, Faint, Medium, Strong, and Very Strong, describing the reaction under UV, not anything you see in normal indoor lighting.

The reputation of fluorescence is more dramatic than its usual effect. A GIA study published in 1997 examined how blue fluorescence changes appearance and found that, for the average observer representing the jewelry-buying public, no systematic effect on appearance was detected. In the face-up position, strongly blue fluorescent diamonds were actually perceived, if anything, to look a touch better in color. Because blue is roughly the complement of the yellow tint in lower color grades, a little blue fluorescence can subtly offset that tint in some stones.

There's one edge case worth knowing. In a small share of stones, very strong fluorescence can make a diamond look slightly hazy, milky, or oily in strong ultraviolet light. It's uncommon, but it's the reason to look at a strongly fluorescent stone yourself rather than rely on the grade alone.

Fluorescence occurs in both mined and Lab Grown Diamonds and is reported the same way for each. Treated as one modest data point rather than a headline, faint or medium fluorescence rarely changes what you see and needn't worry you.

sources.claim: A GIA study published in Gems & Gemology (Winter 1997) found that, for the average observer, blue fluorescence had no systematic effect on diamond appearance, and strongly blue fluorescent diamonds were perceived slightly better in the face-up position.

entries.definition: In 2018 the U.S. Federal Trade Commission revised its Jewelry Guides and removed the word "natural" from the definition of a diamond, formally recognizing that a diamond grown in a laboratory is still a diamond.
entries.body:
The Federal Trade Commission's Jewelry Guides are the rules that govern how diamonds and jewelry can be described and marketed in the United States. For decades the guides defined a diamond, in effect, as a natural mineral made of carbon crystallized in the isometric system. The word "natural" carried an important implication: that only a stone taken from the earth could properly be called a diamond.

In 2018 the FTC revised the guides and removed "natural" from the definition (FTC Jewelry Guides, 2018). The reasoning was straightforward. A Lab Grown Diamond is the same material as a mined one, identical in chemistry, crystal structure, and physical and optical properties, so defining a diamond as something only the earth can make was no longer accurate. Under the revised guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a diamond.

The FTC paired that with guidance on honesty. Sellers should clearly disclose when a stone is a Lab Grown Diamond, not mined, and terms such as "laboratory-grown," "laboratory-created," and "[manufacturer name]-created" are acceptable ways to say so (FTC Jewelry Guides, 2018). The Commission also cautioned against language designed to imply that a Lab Grown Diamond is not a real diamond (FTC Jewelry Guides, 2018).

This is why "a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond" is a statement of regulatory fact, not marketing. The material was always the same; in 2018 the definition was updated to match. Origin remains a genuine and disclosable difference, but it's a difference in where the stone came from, not in what it is.

sources.claim: The FTC revised its Jewelry Guides and removed the word 'natural' from the definition of a diamond.
The FTC advised sellers to disclose lab-grown origin and accepted qualifiers such as 'laboratory-grown', 'laboratory-created', and '[manufacturer name]-created'.
The FTC cautioned that language implying a Lab Grown Diamond is not a real diamond is likely deceptive.

entries.definition: The girdle is the narrow band around the widest part of a diamond, where the crown meets the pavilion. It forms the stone's outline and is where a setting grips it.
entries.body:
The girdle is the dividing line of a diamond. Everything above it is the crown; everything below is the pavilion. Its outline is the shape you name the stone by, round, oval, cushion, and so on, and it is the practical place a jeweler grips the stone, whether with prongs or a surrounding bezel.

Girdle thickness is graded on a scale that runs from extremely thin to extremely thick. A girdle that is too thin can be fragile at the edge and more prone to chipping, while one that is too thick parks extra weight in the rim, where it adds to carat weight without adding much visible size. A medium girdle is usually the goal.

The surface can be finished three ways. A bruted girdle has a frosted, matte look; a polished girdle is smooth and glassy; a faceted girdle carries a ring of tiny facets. Polished and faceted girdles are common on modern stones.

The girdle also gives gemologists their main reference measurement, the average girdle diameter: the average of the stone's own diameter measurements taken at the girdle, its widest point, not the thickness of the girdle band itself. That figure is the denominator used to calculate both table percentage and depth percentage. On many diamonds the report number is laser-inscribed along the girdle, small enough to need magnification to read.

entries.definition: A grading report is a document from an independent gemological laboratory that describes a diamond's measured characteristics, including the 4Cs, on a standard form. It's a scientific description of the stone, not a statement of monetary value or an appraisal.
entries.body:
A typical report lists the shape and cutting style, the measurements in millimeters, the carat weight, the color and clarity grades, and, for round brilliants, a cut grade, along with polish, symmetry, fluorescence, and any laser inscription. Many reports include a plotting diagram that maps the stone's clarity characteristics and a proportions diagram of its cut. The laboratory grades the diamond itself; it doesn't set a price. Value depends on the market and belongs to an appraisal, which is a separate document.

The point of an independent report is consistency. Because a respected lab grades to a published, repeatable standard, a G-color VS1 from one report should mean the same thing as a G-color VS1 from another. That lets you compare stones described by different sellers on equal terms, which is simply good practice, the same way you'd want the specifications on any considered purchase.

For Lab Grown Diamonds the report also states, clearly, that the stone is laboratory-grown, and the same 4C characteristics are measured. IGI issues full 4C reports for Lab Grown Diamonds. GIA, which grades both mined and laboratory-grown stones, moved its Lab Grown Diamond reports to a Premium and Standard tier structure on October 1, 2025, changing how it presents those results.

A practical tip: match the report to the stone. Reputable labs laser-inscribe the report number on the girdle, so you or your jeweler can confirm under magnification that the document in your hand describes the diamond in front of you, a simple check rather than a sign of doubt about anyone.

sources.claim: IGI issues full 4C grading reports for Lab Grown Diamonds.
GIA moved its Lab Grown Diamond reports to a Premium and Standard tier structure on October 1, 2025.

entries.definition: A half bezel, also called a semi-bezel or open bezel, wraps metal around only two sides of the diamond, usually the left and right, leaving the other two sides open. It shields the stone's long sides while letting in more light than a full bezel.
entries.body:
A half bezel is the middle ground between a full bezel and prongs. Two curved walls of metal cup the sides of the stone and hold it firmly, while the top and bottom, or the two ends of an elongated stone, stay open. That open space lets light enter from more directions, so a half-bezeled diamond usually looks brighter and a little larger than one in a full bezel, while the metal on the sides still braces a long stretch of the girdle.

This setting works especially well for elongated shapes, whether worn lengthwise or east-west, because the metal can brace the long sides while leaving the ends open to show the stone's full length. It is a practical pick for someone who wants more protection and less upkeep than prongs give, without fully framing the stone in metal. As with a full bezel, there are no prong tips to catch or wear, which makes a half bezel low-fuss for daily wear. The open edges do leave the uncovered corners or ends more exposed, so for a pointed shape a jeweler will often combine a half bezel on the sides with a V-tip guarding each point. Half bezels can be finished plain, with milgrain, or with a thin bright-cut edge, and they pair naturally with clean, modern ring designs. When you compare it to a full bezel, you are trading a bit of edge coverage for more light and a slightly larger look.

entries.definition: A halo is a ring of small diamonds set around a larger center stone, framing it with a circle of sparkle that makes the center look bigger and brighter. It is one of the most popular center-stone settings.
entries.body:
A halo surrounds the center diamond with a continuous border of small accent stones, usually pave-set, so the frame reads as one bright ring of light. The main reason people choose a halo is size and sparkle for the money: the border adds visual weight, so a center stone can look noticeably larger than its carat weight alone would suggest, and the extra facets throw more light. A halo also lets a smaller or more modestly graded center stone present with more presence, and it can echo the shape of the center, such as a round halo around a round stone or a cushion-shaped halo around a cushion, or deliberately contrast it.

There are variations. A double halo adds a second ring for even more scale. A hidden halo tucks the accent stones under the center stone so the frame shows only in profile. The trade-offs are the same as for any pave work: the small stones are held by tiny beads and can occasionally need re-setting over years of wear, which is routine maintenance, and the busier design has more metal and more seams, so the quality of the setting work matters to how seamless the halo looks. Halos suit round, oval, cushion, and emerald centers especially well, and they pair naturally with pave bands. If you want the center stone's outline to stand completely on its own, a solitaire or a hidden halo is the cleaner choice.

entries.definition: Hearts and arrows is a pattern of eight symmetrical arrows seen face-up and eight hearts seen from the back, visible in a round brilliant cut with very precise optical symmetry and viewed through a special scope. It is a sign of exacting cut symmetry, not a grade of its own.
entries.body:
The pattern comes from the way a round brilliant's facets reflect one another. When the eight pavilion main facets and the eight crown facets are cut to matching angles and spaced evenly, their reflections line up into eight arrowheads when you look straight down through the table, and into eight heart shapes when you look up through the pavilion. Any drift in facet angle, size, or placement smears the shapes, so a clean, crisp hearts and arrows pattern signals that the stone was cut with tight three-dimensional symmetry.

You need a simple viewer to see it. A hearts and arrows scope is a small tube that shines even, colored light onto the stone so the reflections stand out. The arrows are usually visible face-up, while the hearts require viewing from the back, so they are checked before the diamond is set.

The pattern was first observed in Japan in the 1980s and spread to the United States and Europe over the following decade. It applies specifically to round brilliants, because the eightfold arrangement of that cut is what produces the shapes. Fancy shapes, including the elongated hexagonal Dutch Marquise, do not show hearts and arrows.

It helps to be precise about what the pattern does and does not tell you. Excellent optical symmetry usually tracks with strong light performance, but hearts and arrows is a symmetry indicator, not a direct measurement of light return, and a stone can show good hearts and arrows and still be cut to angles that leak some light. Treat it as one useful, checkable quality signal among several. The pattern behaves the same in a mined or Lab Grown Diamond.

sources.claim: The hearts and arrows pattern was first observed in Japan during the 1980s, seen in round brilliants of exceptional symmetry through a special viewer, and spread to the United States and Europe over the following decade.
Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically the same material as a mined diamond.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: A hexagon cut is a diamond with a six-sided outline. It is a geometric fancy shape that can be faceted with long parallel facets, in the brilliant style, or as a mix of the two, and can be cut as a regular hexagon or elongated.
entries.body:
A hexagon cut is defined by its outline: six straight sides meeting at six corners. Beyond that, it is an open category. A hexagon can be faceted with long, flat facets stacked parallel to the girdle for a clean, geometric look, or in the brilliant style for more sparkle, or a blend of the two, so two stones both called hexagons can behave very differently in the light. Hexagons can be close to regular, with all sides similar in length, or elongated, with two longer sides and pointed or angled ends.

Face-up, a hexagon reads as a distinctly modern, architectural shape, and elongated versions flatter the finger the way other stretched shapes do. Because it is a fancy, non-standard cut, it is often chosen for exactly that unusual character.

The vulnerabilities depend on the specific outline. The corners where the straight sides meet, and especially the narrower ends of an elongated hexagon, are the thinnest and most exposed areas, so they benefit from protective setting. Hexagons are frequently set in bezels, which wrap and shield the whole edge, or with prongs placed to cover the corners.

The practical note is that hexagon is a shape, not a standardized cut with fixed proportions, so faceting style, symmetry, and length-to-width ratio all vary widely. It is worth looking at the individual stone to understand how it handles light. The elongated hexagon with pointed ends and straight sides is also the outline behind the trade name Dutch Marquise; the name describes that outline only and does not specify a faceting style.

entries.definition: A hidden halo is a ring of tiny diamonds set on the underside of the center stone's setting, circling the base where it meets the band, so the extra sparkle shows only from the side and profile, not when you look straight down at the ring.
entries.body:
A hidden halo puts a halo where you do not expect one. Instead of framing the center diamond on top, the small stones ring the outside of the basket or collar that holds the center stone, sitting just below it. From directly above, the center diamond looks like a clean solitaire with no frame. Turn the ring to the side, though, and a band of light peeks out around the base, adding a hidden flash of sparkle and a bit of visual lift.

People choose a hidden halo when they want the crisp, uncluttered look of a solitaire from the top but still want the extra brilliance and the more detailed profile that a halo gives. It also adds sparkle without visually enlarging the outline of the center stone the way a traditional halo does, so the center shape stays pure. Because the accent stones sit low and close to the finger, they are fairly protected, though like any small pave-set stones they are held by tiny beads or prongs and benefit from an occasional check. A hidden halo does raise the center stone a little higher off the finger, which is worth knowing if you prefer a low profile or wear gloves often. It pairs with most center shapes, including round, oval, cushion, and elongated cuts, and it is a common way to dress up a design without changing how the center stone reads from above.

entries.definition: HPHT (High Pressure High Temperature) is a method of growing diamond by dissolving carbon in molten metal and letting it crystallize onto a diamond seed under intense pressure and heat, recreating the kind of conditions under which diamonds form deep in the earth.
entries.body:
HPHT recreates, inside a machine, the pressure and heat that form diamonds underground. A large press squeezes a sealed growth cell to enormous pressure while heating it to a high temperature. Inside sits a carbon source, usually graphite, along with a molten metal that acts as a solvent, commonly iron, nickel, or cobalt. The heat dissolves the carbon into the liquid metal, and the dissolved carbon travels through the melt to a cooler zone where a small diamond seed is placed. There the carbon crystallizes onto the seed, and a diamond grows.

The result is a real diamond, chemically and physically identical to a mined one. HPHT-grown crystals often take a distinctive shape with both cube and octahedron faces, and they frequently contain a little nitrogen, which can lend a faint yellow tone. Growers can adjust the recipe to control that.

HPHT does double duty in the diamond world. Besides growing diamonds from scratch, the same high-pressure, high-temperature environment is used as a post-growth treatment to improve the color of already-grown stones, and to treat some mined diamonds as well. When a Lab Grown Diamond has been color-treated this way, a reputable grading report says so.

The two growth methods, HPHT and CVD, reach the same result by different means. HPHT builds the crystal in a metal melt under earth-like pressure; CVD assembles it from gas at low pressure. Both produce genuine diamonds, graded on the same terms.

entries.definition: Inclusions in a Lab Grown Diamond are the tiny internal features, such as metallic flecks, dark crystals, or faint growth lines, that form while the diamond grows. They're the Lab Grown Diamond counterpart to the inclusions in mined diamonds, and they're what graders read to judge clarity and to confirm how a stone was made.
entries.body:
Every diamond has an interior, and most have small features inside called inclusions. Lab Grown Diamonds are no exception. What differs is the kind of inclusion, because it reflects how the stone was grown.

HPHT diamonds grow inside a molten metal, so they can carry tiny metallic flux inclusions, specks of the iron, nickel, or cobalt solvent trapped as the crystal formed. These can look like small dark or reflective flecks, and when there are enough of them, some HPHT stones will respond to a strong magnet, which is one telltale gemologists use. CVD diamonds, grown from gas, tend instead to show faint dark graphitic pinpoints or subtle growth striations, the layered banding left as the crystal built up. Under magnification these features help a lab identify a diamond as grown rather than mined, and by which method.

The same clarity scale and 10x magnification standard apply to grown and mined stones alike, though the report format can differ by lab: IGI issues full 4C reports for Lab Grown Diamonds, clarity included (IGI, 2026), while GIA now places Lab Grown Diamonds in descriptive Premium and Standard tiers rather than issuing an individual clarity grade (GIA, 2025-10-01). At the clarity grades most people buy, inclusions are microscopic and don't affect how the diamond looks to the eye or how it wears.

It's worth saying plainly: inclusions aren't flaws to be nervous about. They're the normal internal features of a real crystal, and they're part of how an independent lab verifies exactly what a stone is and how it was made. Reading them is the lab doing its job, and the report hands you the result.

sources.claim: IGI issues full 4C grading reports for Lab Grown Diamonds, including clarity.
GIA places Lab Grown Diamonds in descriptive Premium and Standard tiers rather than issuing individual 4C grades.

entries.definition: Insuring an engagement ring means buying coverage that pays to repair or replace it if it is lost, stolen, or damaged, either as an add-on to a home or renters policy or as a standalone jewelry policy. You will usually need documentation such as the grading report, the receipt, and an appraisal.
entries.body:
An engagement ring is worn constantly and can be lost, stolen, or damaged, and standard home or renters insurance often limits what it will pay for jewelry. Insurance closes that gap. There are two common routes. The first is a rider, also called a floater or scheduled personal property, added to an existing homeowners or renters policy to raise the coverage on that specific item. The second is a standalone jewelry policy from a company that specializes in it. Standalone policies often cover a wider set of risks, including accidental loss, meaning the stone simply falling out and disappearing, which some riders do not.

To insure a ring you typically provide documentation of what it is and what it costs to replace: the diamond grading report, the purchase receipt, and often a separate insurance appraisal that assigns a replacement value. Read how a policy pays a claim before you buy. Replacement-value coverage pays to replace the ring with one of like kind and quality, agreed-value pays a set figure you settled on up front, and actual-cash-value coverage subtracts for wear and generally pays less.

A few practical questions to ask: Is loss covered or only theft and damage? Is there a deductible? Can you choose where the ring is repaired or replaced, or must you use the insurer's vendor? Does coverage follow you when you travel? Premiums vary with the ring's value, your location, and the coverage, so it is worth getting more than one quote. Keep your documentation and clear photos somewhere separate from the ring itself.

entries.definition: A Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, pure crystallized carbon with the same crystal structure, hardness, and optical properties as a mined diamond, produced in a laboratory instead of forming underground. The only difference is where the carbon crystallized.
entries.body:
A Lab Grown Diamond is not an imitation or a stand-in. It's the same material as a mined diamond: carbon atoms locked into the same cubic crystal lattice, with the same hardness (10 on the Mohs scale), the same brilliance, and the same fire. Set a grown and a mined diamond of matching grades side by side and no one, including a jeweler, can tell them apart by eye. Separating them takes specialized laboratory equipment, which is exactly what the grading labs use.
