There are two ways to grow one, and each has its own entry. HPHT (High Pressure High Temperature) crystallizes carbon under conditions like those deep in the earth. CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) builds the crystal up layer by layer from a carbon-rich gas. Either method produces a genuine diamond; the choice is a matter of process, not of what the stone is.

The recognition is official. In 2018 the U.S. Federal Trade Commission revised its Jewelry Guides and removed the word "natural" from the definition of a diamond, acknowledging that a Lab Grown Diamond is a diamond (FTC Jewelry Guides, 2018). Independent labs grade them: IGI issues full 4C reports for Lab Grown Diamonds (IGI, 2026), while GIA moved Lab Grown Diamonds to descriptive Premium and Standard tiers on October 1, 2025 (GIA, 2025-10-01). An honest seller states plainly that a stone is a Lab Grown Diamond. That origin is a real fact about the stone, not a difference in what the stone is.

sources.claim: The FTC removed the word 'natural' from its definition of a diamond, recognizing that a Lab Grown Diamond is a diamond.
GIA moved Lab Grown Diamonds to descriptive Premium and Standard tiers instead of individual 4C grades.
IGI issues full 4C grading reports for Lab Grown Diamonds.

entries.definition: A Lab Grown Diamond and a mined diamond are the same material, with the same hardness, brilliance, and fire; they differ in origin (a laboratory versus the earth) and usually in price, not in what they are.
entries.body:
Start with what's identical, because most of it is. A Lab Grown Diamond and a mined diamond are both pure carbon in the same crystal structure. Both are a 10 on the Mohs scale, the hardest of any natural material. Both bend and return light the same way, so they show the same brilliance and fire. Both are graded by the same independent labs, and both are diamonds under the FTC's definition (FTC Jewelry Guides, 2018). The report format now differs a little by lab: IGI still issues full 4C reports for Lab Grown Diamonds (IGI, 2026), while GIA grades Lab Grown Diamonds on descriptive Premium and Standard tiers rather than individual 4C grades (GIA, 2025-10-01); mined diamonds still receive individual 4C grades from either lab. No one can tell a mined and a Lab Grown Diamond apart by eye; separating them takes laboratory instruments.

So what actually differs? Two things. The first is origin: one crystallized in the earth over a very long time, the other in a chamber over weeks. That's a real difference, and it's the one an honest seller discloses. Some people care about it, and that preference is legitimate. The second is price. A Lab Grown Diamond is usually less expensive than a mined diamond of similar size and quality, because producing one costs less, not because the stone is lesser.

A diamond, mined or grown, is not an investment. It's a love piece, something you choose and wear because it means something between two people, not an asset to hold for resale. Measuring either kind by what it might fetch later misreads the object. The sound way to choose is simple: pick the origin that means something to you, read the independent report behind the actual stone, and spend what fits your life.

sources.claim: Lab-grown and mined diamonds are both recognized as diamonds under the FTC's revised definition.
IGI issues full 4C grading reports for Lab Grown Diamonds.
GIA grades Lab Grown Diamonds on descriptive Premium and Standard tiers rather than individual 4C grades.

related: Do Lab Grown Diamonds Cloud or Fade?

entries.definition: A laser inscription is text, usually a report number, micro-engraved onto a diamond's girdle with a fine laser and readable under magnification. It lets you match a specific stone to its grading report, and on Lab Grown Diamonds it commonly marks the stone as laboratory-grown.
entries.body:
The girdle is the thin outer edge where the top and bottom of a diamond meet, and it's the standard place for an inscription because it doesn't interfere with how light moves through the stone. The characters are tiny, invisible to the naked eye, and read with a loupe or microscope. Most often the inscription is the grading report number, which ties the physical diamond to its paperwork.

The practical value is simple confirmation. When a lab inscribes the report number on the girdle, you or your jeweler can look under magnification and check that the number matches the report, confirming that the document describes this exact stone. That's a normal, sensible step, the kind of verification any careful buyer appreciates, not a sign of doubt about anyone.

Inscriptions can also carry a brand mark or, at some labs, a short personal message, though the report number is the common case. On laboratory-grown stones the inscription typically includes an origin marker. GIA inscribes "Laboratory-Grown" together with its report number on the girdle of Lab Grown Diamonds. IGI historically inscribed "LAB GROWN" in full and, from early 2023, moved to a shorter "LG" prefix before the report number, where "LG" stands for laboratory-grown. Either way, the report states the origin in plain language, and the inscription is a compact echo of it on the stone itself.

sources.claim: GIA laser-inscribes "Laboratory-Grown" and the report number on the girdle of Lab Grown Diamonds.
IGI historically inscribed "LAB GROWN" on laboratory-grown diamonds and, from early February 2023, moved to an "LG" prefix before the report number.

entries.definition: Length-to-width ratio is a diamond's length divided by its width, both measured across the girdle. It describes how square or elongated the outline is.
entries.body:
Length-to-width ratio comes straight from a diamond's outline measurements: the length in millimeters divided by the width in millimeters, both taken across the girdle. A ratio near 1.00 is square or round; the higher the number, the more elongated the stone.

It is used mainly for fancy (non-round) shapes to describe and compare outlines, and it's a matter of taste, not a quality grade. No ratio is better or worse; it simply sets the look on the hand. Different shapes have conventional ranges most people find balanced. Ovals, pears, and marquises are meant to look long and show higher ratios, while cushions and radiants often sit closer to square. The Dutch Marquise, a trade name for an elongated hexagonal cut with pointed ends and straight, angular sides, typically runs a ratio of roughly 1.5 to 2.0.

The ratio also shapes how a stone reads in wear. A more elongated outline can make a stone look larger for its carat weight from the top, and elongated brilliant cuts commonly show a bow-tie, a soft dark zone across the center that comes from the geometry of stretched shapes. Round brilliants are not given a length-to-width ratio, since they are meant to be circular. Choosing a ratio is about the silhouette you want, not about grading one stone above another.

sources.claim: The Dutch Marquise is an elongated hexagonal cut (a trade name; an IGI report describes it as 'Hexagonal Modified Brilliant') with a length-to-width ratio of roughly 1.5 to 2.0.
sources.source: Stienhardt & Stones Dutch Marquise reference; IGI diamond report

entries.definition: Light performance is an umbrella term for how well a diamond takes in light and returns it to the eye, combining brightness, fire, scintillation, and the absence of light leakage. It is assessed with ray tracing and with viewers like the ASET and the Idealscope rather than by a single universal grade.
entries.body:
Light performance is the practical question behind cut: of all the light that reaches a diamond, how much comes back to your eye as brightness, colored fire, and sparkle, and how much is wasted by leaking out the bottom. A stone with strong light performance returns most of the light it gathers and spreads it evenly across the face. A weak one leaks light, so parts of it look dark, glassy, or dull.

There are two main ways the trade puts numbers to this. GIA's cut grading system, released in January 2006, assigns round brilliants a cut grade from Excellent to Poor based on proportions and on the three appearance effects: brightness, fire, and scintillation. Separately, the American Gem Society Laboratories built a cut grade directly on a ray-traced model of light performance, launched in 2005, scoring light return, leakage, dispersion, and contrast. AGS Laboratories closed at the end of 2022 and merged into GIA, and GIA now offers AGS Ideal light performance reports as a supplement.

You can see light performance without a lab. The Idealscope shows returned light as red, leakage as white, and contrast as black, so a strong stone reads mostly red with a crisp pattern. The ASET (Angular Spectrum Evaluation Tool) maps where a stone gathers its light using red, green, and blue. These are good-practice tools for checking a cut, not signs of doubt about a seller.

Light performance is a matter of cut and the diamond material, so it is identical in principle for mined and Lab Grown Diamonds. It applies most cleanly to round brilliants, where the ideal geometry is best defined.

sources.claim: GIA's round brilliant cut grading system (released January 2006) assigns a cut grade from Excellent to Poor based on proportions and on brightness, fire, and scintillation.
sources.source: GIA (Gemological Institute of America), Diamond Cut grading system, gia.edu / 4cs.gia.edu

sources.claim: The American Gem Society Laboratories launched a cut grade built on a ray-traced light performance model in 2005, scoring light return, leakage, dispersion, and contrast; its ASET (Angular Spectrum Evaluation Tool) maps where a stone gathers light.
sources.source: AGS Laboratories (American Gem Society); PriceScope education

sources.claim: AGS Laboratories closed at the end of 2022 and merged into GIA; GIA began offering AGS Ideal light performance reports as a supplement to its diamond reports in January 2023.
sources.source: National Jeweler; JCK Online (AGS Laboratories to close, merge with GIA)

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically the same material as a mined diamond.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: Made-to-order means your ring is built and set after you place the order, so you can choose the stone, setting, metal, and size, but it takes longer. Ready-to-ship means the ring is already finished and in stock, so it arrives fast with less to customize.
entries.body:
These are two ways a finished ring reaches you, and the difference is mostly time versus choice.

A made-to-order ring, sometimes called built-to-order, is assembled after you buy it. You typically select the center stone, the setting style, the metal, and the finger size, and the piece is set and finished to those specifications. Because a person is mounting the stone and completing the ring for your order, it takes longer, often a couple of weeks or more depending on the maker. The payoff is control: you get the exact combination you want, and you can often choose the specific center diamond by its grading report.

A ready-to-ship ring is already made and sitting in inventory in a set configuration and size. It ships quickly, sometimes within a day or two, which matters if you have a deadline like a planned proposal. The trade-off is less flexibility. You take the stone, setting, and size as offered, though many rings can still be resized afterward.

Which one fits depends on your priorities. If you know exactly what you want, care about picking the individual stone, or want a less common combination, made-to-order is the way, and you should ask for the realistic lead time before you commit. If you are on a tight timeline or you have found a ready-made ring you love, ready-to-ship removes the wait. Either way, ask what is included, how sizing is handled, and what the return or exchange window is, since a made-to-order piece sometimes carries a different return policy than a stock one.

entries.definition: A marquise cut, also called a navette, is an elongated, boat-shaped diamond with two pointed ends and gently curved sides, faceted in the brilliant style. It shows one of the largest face-up spreads per carat of any shape.
entries.body:
The marquise is a modified brilliant with an elongated outline that comes to a point at each end. The sides curve outward from the middle, and brilliant-style facets fill the interior, so it sparkles like a round or an oval rather than flashing like a step cut. It is also called a navette, from the French for a small boat, which its two-pointed outline resembles. Length-to-width ratio varies by cutter, but a marquise is always cut long and narrow, considerably more elongated than an oval, which is what gives it its distinctive silhouette.

Because so much weight is spread across a long, shallow outline, the marquise shows one of the largest face-up areas per carat of any cut, and it elongates the finger dramatically. Looking big for its weight is its main appeal.

The vulnerability is built into the shape. Diamond sits at the top of the Mohs scale, a 10, but hardness resists scratching, not chipping, and the two sharp points at the ends are thin and exposed. A knock in the wrong place can chip a point. For that reason marquise stones are almost always set with protective prongs, often V-shaped ones that cup and shield each tip.

The practical note is orientation and symmetry: the two points must line up exactly across the long axis, because any misalignment is obvious in a shape this linear. A bow-tie shadow across the center is also common, so it is worth viewing the actual stone rather than the report alone.

sources.claim: Diamond is the maximum, a 10, on the Mohs scale of mineral hardness.

entries.definition: The Mohs scale is a 1-to-10 ordinal ranking of a mineral's resistance to being scratched, where a higher-numbered mineral can scratch any lower-numbered one. Diamond sits at 10, the top of the scale.
entries.body:
The Mohs scale was devised in 1812 by the German mineralogist Friedrich Mohs as a simple field test. Take two minerals, try to scratch one with the other, and whichever leaves a mark is the harder. Mohs fixed ten reference minerals as benchmarks, from talc at 1 (soft enough to mark with a fingernail) up through gypsum, calcite, fluorite, apatite, feldspar, quartz at 7, topaz at 8, corundum at 9, and diamond at 10.

Two features of the scale trip people up. First, it measures only scratch resistance, not how hard a stone is to break. A mineral can be very high on Mohs and still chip. Second, the scale is ordinal, not proportional, so the steps are not evenly spaced. The jump in absolute hardness from corundum (9) to diamond (10) is far larger than the jump from quartz (7) to topaz (8). Diamond is not ten percent harder than corundum, it is dramatically harder.

For jewelry, Mohs hardness is a practical durability guide. Anything below about 7 can be dulled over time by everyday dust, which is largely quartz at hardness 7. That is why diamond (10), moissanite (about 9.25), and sapphire (9) wear so well in rings, and why softer stones and simulants show surface wear sooner. Hardness is one axis of durability. Toughness, the resistance to chipping and breaking, is a separate one.

sources.claim: The Mohs hardness scale (an ordinal 1-to-10 scratch scale running talc 1, gypsum 2, calcite 3, fluorite 4, apatite 5, feldspar 6, quartz 7, topaz 8, corundum 9, diamond 10) was devised by the German mineralogist Friedrich Mohs.
sources.source: Friedrich Mohs, Versuch einer Elementar-Methode zur naturhistorischen Bestimmung und Erkennung der Fossilien, per Encyclopaedia Britannica

entries.definition: Moissanite is silicon carbide (SiC), a hard, brilliant crystalline material used both as a diamond simulant and as a gemstone in its own right. Almost all moissanite in jewelry is lab-created, because the natural mineral is vanishingly rare.
entries.body:
Moissanite is not a type of diamond, and it is not a lower grade of anything. It is a separate material, silicon carbide, with its own chemistry and its own look. The French chemist Henri Moissan found small crystals in fragments of a meteorite from Canyon Diablo in Arizona in 1893, and first mistook them for diamond. In 1905, the American mineralogist George Kunz confirmed the crystals were silicon carbide and named the mineral moissanite in Moissan's honor. Moissan himself went on to receive the 1906 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for separate work, isolating the element fluorine and developing the electric furnace that carries his name.

Moissanite is very hard, about 9.25 on the Mohs scale, second among common gem materials only to diamond at 10. Optically it is livelier than diamond in one specific way: its dispersion, the fire or splitting of light into colors, is about 0.104, more than double diamond's 0.044, so larger moissanites can throw noticeably more colored flash. Moissanite is also doubly refractive, meaning it splits light into two rays. Look through the top of a stone at the back facets and you can sometimes see them appear doubled, which never happens in diamond.

Moissanite conducts heat well, well enough that it can read as diamond on an older thermal-only tester. It differs from diamond in electrical conductivity, which is how modern combination testers tell the two apart. None of this makes moissanite a fake diamond. It is an honest, durable gem with a different fingerprint, sold openly as what it is.

sources.claim: Henri Moissan found small crystals in fragments of a meteorite from Canyon Diablo, Arizona, and initially believed them to be diamond.
sources.source: Historical account of Moissan's meteorite analysis, per mineralogical references (Mindat.org, entry for moissanite)

sources.claim: The American mineralogist George Kunz confirmed that the Canyon Diablo crystals were silicon carbide and published the mineral name moissanite in Moissan's honor.
sources.source: G. F. Kunz, "Moissanite, a natural silicon carbide," American Journal of Science, vol. 19, pp. 396-397

sources.claim: Henri Moissan received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for isolating the element fluorine and for the electric furnace named after him.

entries.definition: A moval is a hybrid diamond shape between a marquise and an oval: an elongated brilliant cut with the soft curved sides of an oval and gently pointed, or slightly tapered, ends. Its name is a blend of the words marquise and oval.
entries.body:
The moval sits on the spectrum between two familiar shapes. An oval has fully rounded ends; a marquise has two sharp points. A moval is in between: an elongated modified brilliant with curved sides like an oval, but ends that come to a gentle, softened point rather than a full curve or a sharp tip. The name is simply a blend of marquise and oval, and the look splits the difference too.

Face-up, a moval behaves like other elongated brilliants. It spreads its weight over a long outline, so it reads large for its carat weight, and it lengthens and slims the appearance of the finger. The softly pointed ends give it more shape and direction than a plain oval while avoiding the very sharp, fragile tips of a full marquise.

That is also its durability story. Because the ends are gently rounded points rather than sharp ones, a moval is less prone to chipping than a marquise, though the ends are still the thinnest part of the stone and are usually protected with prongs. A soft bow-tie shadow across the center, common to elongated brilliants, can appear here too.

The practical note is that the moval is a fancy, less common shape, so selection is limited and there is no fixed standard for exactly how pointed the ends should be. It is worth viewing the specific stone, since the balance between oval softness and marquise point varies from cutter to cutter and is the whole reason to choose this shape.

entries.definition: An old European cut is an antique round brilliant: a circular, hand-cut diamond with a high crown, a small table, a deep pavilion, and a large flat culet. It is the direct ancestor of the modern round brilliant.
entries.body:
The old European cut is the round member of the antique brilliant family and the closest predecessor to today's round brilliant. Like the modern round it has a circular outline and roughly the same count of brilliant-style facets, but its proportions come from the hand-cutting era: a tall crown, a small table, a steep and deep pavilion, and a large culet that shows face-up as a small open circle at the very center of the stone.

Face-up, an old European cut looks warmer and chunkier than a modern round. It was cut for candlelight and soft indoor light rather than bright modern lighting, so it returns light in broad, bold flashes with more visible fire and less of the small, uniform sparkle a modern round is engineered to produce. That distinct older character is exactly why many people seek these stones out.

Its round outline has no points or corners, so like a modern round it is a durable, secure shape to set.

The practical note is that old European cuts predate standardized, machine-assisted cutting, so their symmetry is often slightly irregular and each stone is individual. They are best appreciated on their own terms rather than judged against modern ideal-cut light-return standards, which they were never cut to meet. As with old mine cuts, new stones are sometimes cut today in the old European style, so ask whether a given stone is a genuine antique or a modern interpretation if that distinction matters to you.

entries.definition: An old mine cut is an antique brilliant cut with a squarish, cushion-like outline, a high crown, a small table, a deep pavilion, and a large flat culet at the bottom. It is an early, hand-cut ancestor of the modern cushion and round brilliants.
entries.body:
The old mine cut is a brilliant-family cut from the era of hand cutting, before modern proportions were standardized. Its outline is roughly square with rounded corners, close to a cushion, and its proportions are distinctive: a tall crown, a small table up top, a deep pavilion, and a large culet, the facet polished flat across the bottom point, which is big enough to be visible face-up as a small open window at the center.

Face-up, an old mine cut behaves differently from a modern stone. It was cut to look its best in candlelight and low, warm light, so instead of the bright, uniform pinfire of a modern round it shows broad, bold, softer flashes and a chunky pattern of light. Many people find that older character warm and full of personality.

Because the corners are rounded, an old mine cut is durable and free of the fragile points that chip on pointed shapes.

The honest practical note is that each old mine cut is an individual. These stones were cut by hand, one at a time, so symmetry is often irregular and no two are quite alike, and they should be read on their own terms rather than against modern ideal-cut expectations. Modern cutters also make new stones in the old mine style, so if genuine age matters to you, it is worth asking whether a given stone is an antique or a contemporary cut in the antique manner.

entries.definition: An oval cut is an elongated, rounded diamond with brilliant-style faceting, essentially a round brilliant stretched along one axis. It keeps much of a round's sparkle while covering more of the finger.
entries.body:
The oval is a modified brilliant: it uses the same family of triangular and kite-shaped facets as a round brilliant, arranged around an elongated closed curve with no points or corners. Length-to-width ratio is a matter of taste, but most ovals are cut somewhere around 1.3 to 1.5 to one.

Face-up, an oval reads larger than a round of the same carat weight, because its outline is stretched and spreads the same weight over a longer surface. That elongation also flatters the hand, making the finger look longer and slimmer. Because the shape has no sharp points, it is durable and easy to set, though the two gently curved ends are still the thinnest part of the stone.

The feature to watch is the bow-tie: a shadow shaped like a bow tie that stretches across the center of many elongated brilliant cuts when light passing through the middle leaks out instead of reflecting back. Almost every oval has some bow-tie, but the severity ranges from nearly invisible to a dark band that dominates the stone.

One practical point: a bow-tie does not show up in the standard grading numbers, so it is worth looking at the actual stone, or a video of it, rather than the report alone. Tilting the stone in the light shows how pronounced the bow-tie is. This is ordinary buying diligence, the sort of look you would give any stone before choosing it.

entries.definition: Pave (pronounced pah-vay, from the French for paved) is a technique that packs many tiny diamonds close together, held by minute metal beads, so the surface reads as continuous sparkle rather than individual stones. Micro-pave uses especially small stones.
entries.body:
In pave, a setter drills seats for a row or field of small diamonds and raises tiny beads of metal from the surrounding surface to hold each stone at its corners. Because the stones sit close and the beads are minute, the eye sees a paved sheet of light rather than separate settings, which is where the name comes from. Pave most often runs along the shoulders of a band, but it can also cover the sides of a setting, the halo around a center stone, or the whole surface of a design.

Pave adds a great deal of sparkle for relatively little carat weight, and it makes a center stone look larger by ringing it with light. The trade-off is care. The stones are held by small beads, so an occasional pave diamond can loosen or fall out over years of hard wear. This is a normal maintenance item, not a defect, and a jeweler can reset a lost stone easily. Having the beads looked at when you get your prongs checked keeps a pave ring in good shape. Because the band surface is filled with stones, a fully paved band is harder to resize without disturbing them, so it is often made in a fixed size or sized only in a plain section. Related styles include micro-pave, which uses even smaller stones for a smoother field of light, and channel setting, where small stones sit between two walls of metal instead of under beads.

entries.definition: The pavilion is the lower part of a diamond, the cone-shaped section below the girdle that tapers toward the culet. Its angled facets reflect light back up toward your eye.
entries.body:
The pavilion is where a diamond gets most of its brightness. Its angled facets act as internal mirrors: light that comes in through the table and crown strikes the pavilion walls and is reflected back up and out toward your eye. When the pavilion angle is close to ideal, most of that light returns through the top and the stone looks bright and lively.

When the angle is off, light escapes instead of returning. A pavilion cut too shallow lets light leak out the bottom and can create a ring-shaped reflection called a fish-eye. A pavilion cut too deep sends light out the sides and leaves a dark area in the center, sometimes called a nailhead. Pavilion depth, given as a percentage of the stone's width, is the proportion that captures this, and it is read alongside the pavilion angle itself.

On a standard round brilliant the pavilion carries twenty-four facets, twenty-five if you count a culet facet at the tip. Because the pavilion sits down inside the mounting, it's largely hidden once the stone is set, so its cut and condition are best judged beforehand. Simply put, the crown lets light in and makes color, and the pavilion is the reflector that sends light back to you.

entries.definition: A pear cut is a teardrop-shaped diamond with one softly rounded end and one point, faceted in the brilliant style. It reads as half oval and half marquise, and is also called a teardrop cut.
entries.body:
The pear is a modified brilliant: brilliant-style triangular and kite facets fill a closed outline that is round at one end and tapers to a single point at the other. In effect it is half oval and half marquise, and it is conventionally worn with the point facing up the hand, toward the fingernail.

Face-up, the pear elongates the finger like other stretched shapes and shows a generous spread for its carat weight. The rounded end carries most of the visual weight, while the point adds length and a sense of direction. A gentle bow-tie shadow across the widest part of the stone is common, as it is in ovals and marquises.

The vulnerability is the single point. It is the thinnest, most exposed part of the stone, and while diamond is extremely hard, a sharp tip can still chip on a hard impact, so the point is protected with a prong, usually a V-shaped one that cups it.

The practical note is symmetry. The point should sit exactly in line with the high center of the rounded end, and the two shoulders, the curved sides near the wide end, should mirror each other. Because a pear is not symmetrical top to bottom, a stone that is even slightly lopsided looks off once it is set, so it is worth checking the outline against the report before choosing. Length-to-width ratio also varies, from a rounded, stubby pear to a long, slender one, and that is purely preference.

entries.definition: Platinum is a naturally white, dense, and durable precious metal used for fine jewelry, typically alloyed to about 90 to 95 percent purity. Unlike white gold, its white color is natural and permanent, so it never needs rhodium plating.
entries.body:
Platinum is a premium setting metal prized for three things: color, density, and security. Its color is a true, cool white that comes from the metal itself, not from a coating, so a platinum ring stays white for life with no replating. Jewelry platinum is usually marked 950 or 900, meaning 95 or 90 percent pure platinum, a higher precious-metal content than even 18k gold. That purity also makes it a good option for people with metal sensitivities, since it contains little or no nickel.

Platinum is denser and heavier than gold, so a platinum ring has a noticeable, substantial weight on the hand. It is also excellent for holding diamonds: platinum prongs are strong and keep their grip well, which is why many jewelers favor it for securing valuable center stones and delicate pointed shapes. Platinum does scratch, but it wears differently from gold. Instead of losing metal, it mostly displaces, developing a soft matte surface called a patina over time. Some people love the patina; others prefer to have a jeweler polish the ring back to a high shine periodically. Either way, very little metal is actually lost. The trade-offs are cost and workability. Platinum is denser, so a ring uses more grams of metal, and the metal is harder to work, which is why platinum pieces usually cost more to make than comparable gold ones. It can also be a little more involved to resize, though a good jeweler resizes it routinely. For a white metal that never needs replating and holds stones securely, platinum is the durable, low-maintenance choice.

entries.definition: A Portuguese cut is a highly faceted round cut with multiple rows of triangular facets stacked above and below the girdle, giving it far more facets than a standard round brilliant and an intense, lattice-like sparkle. It is a specialty cut, more common in colored gemstones but used for diamonds as well.
entries.body:
The Portuguese cut is defined by its dense, layered faceting. Where a standard round brilliant has a single main row of facets on the crown and pavilion, a Portuguese cut adds extra rows of triangular and rhombus-shaped facets, typically two or more tiers on each half of the stone. The result is a much higher facet count and a complex, starburst or lattice pattern of light. The outline is usually round, though the same faceting scheme is sometimes applied to other shapes.

Face-up, all that faceting produces intense, fine-grained scintillation: many small, busy flashes rather than a few broad ones. The extra rows scatter light in more directions, which is why the style is prized for lively sparkle, especially in transparent colored gemstones, where it is most often seen.

On durability the Portuguese cut behaves like any round: its curved outline has no points or corners to chip, so it is a secure, forgiving shape to set.

The honest practical note is that the Portuguese cut is a labor-intensive specialty. All those extra facets take more time and skill to cut, and the style is far more common in quartz, tourmaline, and other gemstones than in diamond, so as a diamond it is an uncommon, deliberate choice rather than a stock shape. If you want one in diamond, expect limited availability and to seek out a cutter or seller who offers it specifically.

entries.definition: Post-growth treatment is a process applied to a Lab Grown Diamond after it's grown to improve its color, most often HPHT annealing, in which the finished crystal is exposed to high pressure and high temperature that shift its color toward colorless. The change is permanent and is disclosed on the grading report.
entries.body:
Some Lab Grown Diamonds, particularly those made by CVD, come out of growth with a faint brown or gray cast. The color comes from the way atoms and tiny gaps are arranged in the crystal as it forms. Post-growth treatment corrects this. The most common form is HPHT annealing: the finished diamond is placed under high pressure and high temperature, the same kind of environment used to grow HPHT diamonds, which rearranges those atomic-scale features and clears the tint, moving the stone toward colorless.

Two things are worth understanding plainly. First, the change is permanent and stable. HPHT annealing alters the crystal itself, not a coating on the surface, so the improved color doesn't wear off, wash away, or revert under normal conditions. Second, it doesn't make the stone any less of a diamond. It's still pure crystallized carbon with the same hardness; the treatment only adjusts color.

Treatment is a normal, well-understood part of diamond production, for grown and mined stones alike, and the standard is transparency. Independent grading reports note when a diamond has been treated, so the information travels with the stone. Checking the report to see how a diamond was grown and whether it was treated is simply good practice, the same way you'd read the details on anything you're buying. It's a way to know exactly what you have, not a reason for worry.

entries.definition: A princess cut is a square diamond with sharp, uncut corners and brilliant-style faceting, technically a square modified brilliant. It offers a lot of sparkle in a clean, contemporary square outline.
entries.body:
The princess is a modified brilliant with a square, sometimes slightly rectangular, outline. Unlike the emerald, Asscher, and radiant, its four corners are left sharp and pointed rather than cut off. Inside, it uses brilliant-style faceting, with chevron-shaped facets on the pavilion that create bright, fiery sparkle across the whole face.

Face-up, a princess reads as a crisp square full of light, a modern look that pairs well with clean, geometric settings. Because a square outline packs weight efficiently, a princess also tends to be gentler on budget than a round of the same carat weight, partly because a princess can be cut from a rough crystal with less waste.

The vulnerability lives in those four sharp corners. Diamond sits at 10 on the Mohs scale, the top of the scale, but hardness measures resistance to scratching, not to chipping, and a pointed corner is the most exposed part of the stone. A knock can chip a corner, so a princess should always be set with prongs that cover and protect all four corners, or in a bezel or half-bezel that shields them.

The practical note is to make sure the setting genuinely protects the corners, since that is the one real weakness of an otherwise durable and brilliant shape. It is also worth checking that the stone is a true square if that is the look you want, because many princess cuts are very slightly rectangular.

sources.claim: Diamond is a 10, the maximum, on the Mohs scale of mineral hardness.

entries.definition: A prong setting holds a diamond with several thin metal claws that grip the stone near its girdle and lift it up so light can reach it from all sides. It is the most common way to set a center stone.
entries.body:
A prong setting, also called a claw setting, uses three or more slender metal fingers that rise from the base of the setting and bend over the edge of the stone to lock it in place. The most common counts are four prongs and six prongs. Four prongs show more of the diamond and let in more light, while six prongs hold the stone more securely and make a round diamond look rounder. Because the metal touches the stone in only a few small spots, a prong setting exposes the most surface to light, which is why it is the standard choice for maximizing brilliance.

The trade-off is protection. Prongs sit above the surface of the ring, so they can snag on fabric and hair, and the tips can wear down or bend over years of daily wear. This is normal and expected, not a flaw. A jeweler can re-tip or rebuild worn prongs, and having them checked once or twice a year is good routine care. Shapes with pointed corners, such as marquise, pear, princess, heart, and the Dutch Marquise, need a special V-shaped prong to shield the point, since an exposed point is the most vulnerable part of any stone. Round, oval, and cushion diamonds are forgiving in ordinary prongs because they have no sharp corners to chip. When you choose a prong setting, weigh how much light and visibility you want against how much the setting sits up off the finger.

entries.definition: A radiant cut is a rectangular or square diamond with cut corners and brilliant-style faceting. It combines the trimmed corners of an emerald cut with the sparkling, triangular-facet interior of a round, which makes it a mixed cut.
entries.body:
The radiant is the bridge between the two main faceting families. Its outline looks like an emerald cut, a rectangle or square with the corners cropped off, but its interior is faceted in the brilliant style, with many small triangular and kite facets instead of long step-cut rows. That combination is why it is called a mixed cut, and it is what gives a radiant far more sparkle than an emerald or Asscher of the same outline.

Face-up, a radiant is lively and bright, and the busy faceting has a useful side effect: it breaks light up so thoroughly that it tends to disguise body color and small inclusions better than an open step cut does. A radiant can therefore look white and clean at a slightly lower color or clarity grade. Radiants come in square and elongated versions, and the elongated ones flatter the finger the way any stretched shape does.

On durability the radiant is one of the sturdier fancy shapes, because the cropped corners remove the sharp points that chip on other cuts, letting it sit securely in a setting.

One honest caveat: radiant is a broad label. The faceting pattern and how chunky or crushed the sparkle looks vary quite a bit from stone to stone, and length-to-width ratio ranges from square to distinctly long. It is worth viewing a specific radiant face-up to see whether its particular sparkle and proportions suit you.

entries.definition: Refractive index (n) is a number that says how much a material slows and bends light compared with a vacuum. Diamond's is about 2.417, among the highest of any common transparent material, and it is the root cause of a diamond's brightness.
entries.body:
Light travels fastest in a vacuum. In any transparent material it slows down, and the ratio of the vacuum speed to the speed inside the material is the refractive index, written n. A higher n means light slows more and bends more sharply when it crosses the surface at an angle. For diamond, n is about 2.417 (measured with yellow sodium light near 589 nanometers). For comparison, window glass is near 1.5, water is 1.33, sapphire is about 1.77, and cubic zirconia is near 2.15. Moissanite is higher than diamond, near 2.65, and unlike diamond it is doubly refractive, splitting light into two rays.

A high refractive index does two things that make diamond bright. First, more light reflects at the surface, which adds surface sparkle. Second, and more important, a high n produces a small critical angle, the cutoff beyond which light striking a facet from the inside reflects completely back into the stone instead of passing through. Diamond's critical angle is only about 24.4 degrees, so light that enters is easily trapped and sent back toward the viewer. That is the physics a good cut exploits.

Refractive index is a fixed property of the material. It cannot be changed by cutting, and it is identical in mined and Lab Grown Diamonds because both are the same carbon crystal. Gemologists use it to separate diamond from look-alikes, though not with a standard refractometer: diamond's index is higher than the roughly 1.81 ceiling those instruments read, so diamond and its highest-index simulants simply read off the scale. Instead, a reflectivity meter estimates the index from how much light reflects off the stone, or a thermal or electrical probe checks a different property entirely. That is a routine, good-practice identity check, not a sign of doubt about any particular stone.

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically identical to a mined diamond, so its refractive index is the same.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: Ring sizing is measuring the finger to find the right band size, and resizing is a jeweler's later adjustment of an existing ring up or down to fit. Most plain metal bands resize easily, while rings with stones all the way around, or certain designs, may resize little or not at all.
entries.body:
Ring size is expressed on a numbered scale, in the United States as whole and half sizes with quarter sizes available, that corresponds to the inside circumference of the band. Fingers change through the day and the year: they swell in heat and shrink in cold, so the best measurement is taken when your hands are at a normal, warm temperature, not first thing on a cold morning. Aim for a fit that is snug enough not to spin freely but loose enough to slide over the knuckle. When in doubt, a jeweler can measure precisely with a set of sizing rings.

Resizing is routine for a plain band or a ring with stones only on the top. A jeweler cuts the shank, adds or removes a small piece of metal, then solders and finishes it, usually within a size or two in either direction without trouble. Some designs resist resizing. A full eternity band or a band set with pave or channel-set stones all the way around cannot easily be cut, and tension settings and certain patterned bands are also hard to adjust, so those are best ordered in the correct size or built with a comfort solution like sizing beads or a hinged shank. Metals differ too: gold and platinum resize well, while some contemporary metals cannot be resized at all. If a ring is only slightly loose, sizing beads or a plastic guard can help without a full resize. When you buy, it is good practice to ask up front how sizable a given design is, so future adjustments are easy to plan for, since fingers do change over a lifetime.

entries.definition: A round brilliant cut is a circular diamond with 57 or 58 facets arranged in the brilliant style, triangular and kite-shaped facets radiating from the center, to maximize light return. It is the most common diamond cut and the benchmark against which other shapes are compared.
entries.body:
The round brilliant belongs to the brilliant faceting family, meaning its facets are triangular and kite-shaped and fan out from the center rather than running in straight parallel rows. A standard round brilliant has 57 facets, or 58 if a small facet called a culet is polished onto the point at the bottom. The modern proportions that make a round return so much light were set out in the mathematical work of Marcel Tolkowsky in 1919.

Face-up, the round returns more light than any other shape, and its symmetry makes it the most forgiving cut to grade and to buy. Because the outline is a circle with no points or corners, it is also the most durable shape: there is no thin, exposed tip to catch on a hard surface. That durability is why rounds are set in the widest range of styles, from a simple solitaire to a protective bezel.

The honest practical note is cost. A round brilliant uses a smaller share of the original rough crystal than most fancy shapes, and demand for it is the highest of any cut, so a round typically costs more per carat than an equivalent oval, cushion, or emerald cut of similar quality. The cut geometry is identical whether a diamond is mined or Lab Grown, and a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond (FTC Jewelry Guides, 2018), so the same proportions apply to both.

sources.claim: The modern round brilliant's light-optimizing proportions were set out by Marcel Tolkowsky in Diamond Design.
A Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond.

entries.definition: Scintillation is a diamond's sparkle: the play of light and dark flashes seen as the stone, the light, or the viewer moves. In GIA's cut model it covers both the flashes of light and the pattern of light and dark areas across the stone.
entries.body:
Brilliance and fire describe light returning at a single instant. Scintillation is about change over time. As a diamond tips and turns, each facet switches between catching a light source and falling into shadow, so the stone flickers with quick flashes. Those flashes are the sparkle. The second half of scintillation is the pattern: the arrangement of bright and dark facets you see face-up when the stone is held still. Contrast between light and dark facets is not a flaw. It gives the eye distinct spots to catch as the stone moves, which makes the sparkle read as crisp rather than washed out.

Scintillation depends on the number, size, and arrangement of facets and on the quality of polish and symmetry. A standard round brilliant has 57 or 58 facets, an arrangement worked out to balance the size and spacing of the flashes. More facets, or smaller ones, change the character of the sparkle, making it finer and busier or broader and bolder.

Lighting and motion matter as much as the stone. Scintillation only shows itself with multiple light sources or a moving viewer, which is why diamonds sparkle most in a room with several small lights rather than under one flat, even source.

GIA's cut grading system, released in January 2006, treats scintillation as one of three appearance components, along with brightness and fire, in setting a round brilliant's cut grade from Excellent to Poor. Scintillation is a function of cut and material, not origin, so a well-cut Lab Grown Diamond scintillates the same as a well-cut mined diamond.

sources.claim: GIA's round brilliant cut grading system (released January 2006) treats scintillation as one of three face-up appearance components, alongside brightness and fire, in a cut grade from Excellent to Poor.
sources.source: GIA (Gemological Institute of America), Diamond Cut grading system, gia.edu / 4cs.gia.edu

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically the same material as a mined diamond.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: A synthetic diamond (a Lab Grown Diamond) is the same material as a mined diamond, grown in a lab instead of forming in the Earth. A simulant is a different material that merely looks like diamond. In short: a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, a simulant is not.
entries.body:
This is the single most useful distinction in the whole subject, and it is often blurred. Synthetic and simulant are not synonyms.

A synthetic diamond, better called a Lab Grown Diamond, has the identical chemistry (pure carbon), crystal structure, hardness, and optical properties of a mined diamond. It is grown by high-pressure high-temperature (HPHT) or chemical vapor deposition (CVD) methods instead of forming in the Earth, but it is diamond in every physical sense. U.S. regulators make this official. The FTC's 2018 Jewelry Guides define a diamond by its composition and dropped the word natural, so a Lab Grown Diamond is a diamond and must be disclosed as such, never passed off as mined.

A simulant, also called an imitation, is a different material chosen because it resembles diamond to the eye. Cubic zirconia, moissanite, white sapphire, YAG (yttrium aluminum garnet), and even glass are all diamond simulants. They can look similar, but they differ in hardness, density, heat and electrical behavior, and fire, which is exactly how they are identified.

The practical takeaway is simple. Lab Grown Diamond and simulant describe completely different things. A Lab Grown Diamond is a diamond that happens to be grown rather than mined. A simulant is not a diamond at all, whatever it looks like. Good sellers state plainly which one a stone is, and the terms above are the words to look for.

sources.claim: The FTC's revised Jewelry Guides define a diamond by composition and require Lab Grown Diamonds to be clearly disclosed as such, while simulants (imitations) must be disclosed as not being diamonds.
sources.source: FTC, Guides for the Jewelry, Precious Metals, and Pewter Industries (Federal Register)

entries.definition: A solitaire is a ring set with a single diamond and no accent stones. The name refers to that one solitary stone, and it is the classic, most recognizable engagement ring style.
entries.body:
A solitaire puts all the attention on one diamond. The setting, most often a prong setting but sometimes a bezel, holds the single stone on a plain metal band with no side stones, halo, or pave. Because nothing competes with it, the center stone's cut, shape, and proportions carry the whole look, so a solitaire rewards a well-cut stone and hides nothing.

The appeal is timelessness and clarity. A solitaire never looks dated, it is easy to clean because there are few crevices, and it usually costs less in setting labor and side stones than a more elaborate design, which lets more of the budget go toward the center stone itself. The plain band is also the easiest style to resize later. The trade-offs are that a solitaire offers no accent stones to add sparkle or apparent size, so the center stone stands entirely on its own, and a single stone in raised prongs sits up where it can snag, which is why prong count and, for pointed shapes, V-tips matter. If you want more sparkle or a larger look, a halo or a pave band builds on the same idea. If you want maximum protection, a bezel solitaire keeps the clean single-stone look while shielding the edges. A solitaire suits every diamond shape and any metal, and it holds any diamond equally well, including Lab Grown Diamonds, which are real diamonds with the same composition and properties as mined ones.

sources.claim: Lab Grown Diamonds have essentially the same optical, physical, and chemical properties as mined diamonds and are properly called diamonds; the U.S. Federal Trade Commission recognized this in its revised Jewelry Guides.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, Guides for the Jewelry, Precious Metals, and Pewter Industries (revised)

entries.definition: The table is the large, flat facet on the top of a diamond. It is the single biggest facet on the stone and sits at the center of the crown.
entries.body:
The table is the main window into a diamond. Most light enters and exits through it, so it does a lot of the work of showing brightness. On a round brilliant the table is an eight-sided facet at the very center of the top; on step cuts like the emerald cut it is a rectangle.

Its size relative to the width of the stone is what gemologists track, and they express it as table percentage. The trade-off is simple to picture. A larger table opens up the center and shows more of the bright light returning straight up from the pavilion, but it leaves less room for the small angled crown facets that split white light into color, so a very large table can look bright but a little flat. A smaller table gives those crown facets more room to produce fire, at the cost of a smaller central window.

Flatness and placement matter too. The table should sit level and centered over the stone. A table that looks tilted or off-center points to a symmetry problem in the cut rather than a separate feature. Because the table is the first surface most people look through, its size and its relationship to the crown and pavilion below are a big part of how lively a finished diamond looks.

entries.definition: Table percentage is the width of a diamond's table facet divided by its average girdle diameter, expressed as a percentage. It describes how large the top window is relative to the whole stone.
entries.body:
Table percentage describes how big a diamond's top window is relative to the whole stone. The formula is the width of the table facet divided by the average girdle diameter, multiplied by one hundred. On a round brilliant the table width is measured straight across the eight-sided table.

The number captures a balance. The flat table returns bright light straight up through the center of the stone, while the smaller angled crown facets around it split light into color. A larger table opens up that bright central return but crowds out the crown facets, so a very high table percentage can read as bright but a little flat and short on fire. A smaller table leaves more room for fire from the crown but shrinks the central window. Because brightness and fire pull against each other, a grading lab weighs table percentage as one factor in the stone's overall proportions rather than judging it on its own, and the flattering range shifts from one shape to another.

In practice, table percentage is always read together with depth percentage, crown angle, and pavilion angle, since those proportions work as a set. Marcel Tolkowsky's 1919 proportions for the round brilliant included a specific table size, one of the early efforts to describe the balance that makes the cut look lively.

sources.claim: Marcel Tolkowsky's 1919 proportions for the round brilliant specified a table size.

entries.definition: The 4Cs are the four standard measures of a polished diamond's quality: carat weight, color, clarity, and cut. They're the shared vocabulary the whole diamond trade uses to describe and compare stones, popularized and standardized by GIA over the twentieth century.
entries.body:
Each C answers a different question. Carat is how much the diamond weighs. Color is how little body color it has, from colorless down to a light tint. Clarity is how free it is of inclusions and blemishes under magnification. Cut is how well its proportions and finish return light. Read together, the four give a compact, comparable picture of a diamond; read alone, any single C can mislead.

The 4Cs are useful because they're shared. Before a common standard existed, a word like fine or good could mean something different depending on who used it. GIA, the nonprofit Gemological Institute of America founded by Robert M. Shipley in 1931, set out to fix that. Shipley popularized the "four Cs" mnemonic in the 1940s as a teaching device, so students could remember the factors that determine quality.

The memorable phrase became a working system in 1953, when GIA's Richard Liddicoat introduced the International Diamond Grading System, including the D-to-Z color scale and the flawless-to-included clarity scale still used worldwide. A cut grade for round brilliants came much later, released by GIA in 2006.

The 4Cs apply the same way to Lab Grown Diamonds, which are graded on the identical carat, color, clarity, and cut standards. A good habit is to weigh the four together, and against your own eye, rather than chasing one top number. The combination, not any single C, is what you actually see and wear.

sources.claim: The Gemological Institute of America (GIA), a nonprofit, was founded by Robert M. Shipley in 1931.
GIA's Robert Shipley popularized the "four Cs" mnemonic in the 1940s as a teaching device.
GIA's Richard Liddicoat introduced the International Diamond Grading System, including the D-to-Z color scale and the clarity scale, in 1953.
GIA released its cut grade for round brilliant diamonds in 2006.
sources.source: Gemological Institute of America (GIA), reported by JCK

entries.definition: The Cullinan is the largest gem-quality rough diamond ever found, at 3,106 carats. Discovered in South Africa in 1905, it was cut into stones that include the two largest diamonds in the British Crown Jewels.
entries.body:
The Cullinan is the largest gem-quality rough diamond ever discovered, weighing 3,106 carats before cutting. It was found in 1905 at the Premier mine near Pretoria, in what is now South Africa, and was named after Thomas Cullinan, the chairman of the mining company. In 1907 the government of the Transvaal bought the stone and presented it to King Edward VII.

The rough was sent to the firm of Joseph Asscher in Amsterdam, where cutting began in 1908. It yielded nine principal stones and a number of smaller ones. The largest, Cullinan I, is also called the Great Star of Africa. At 530.4 carats it is one of the largest cut colorless diamonds in the world, and it is set in the head of the Sovereign's Sceptre with Cross. The second largest, Cullinan II or the Second Star of Africa, weighs 317.4 carats and is set in the Imperial State Crown. Both are part of the British Crown Jewels and are held in the Royal Collection.

The Cullinan is worth knowing for scale. A finished one carat diamond is a common engagement ring size, and the Great Star of Africa alone weighs more than 500 times that. Figures like these are why the stone still anchors almost every list of history's most famous diamonds.

sources.claim: The Cullinan, at 3,106 carats, is the largest gem-quality rough diamond ever found; it was discovered in 1905 at the Premier mine near Pretoria, South Africa.
sources.source: Task anchor (3,106 carats, found 1905); Encyclopaedia Britannica

sources.claim: The diamond was named after Thomas Cullinan, chairman of the mining company, and in 1907 the Transvaal government bought it and presented it to King Edward VII.
The rough was cut by the firm of Joseph Asscher in Amsterdam beginning in 1908 and yielded nine principal stones and a number of smaller ones.

Cullinan I (the Great Star of Africa), 530.4 carats, is set in the Sovereign's Sceptre with Cross; Cullinan II (the Second Star of Africa), 317.4 carats, is set in the Imperial State Crown; both are part of the British Crown Jewels.

entries.definition: The eye-clean strategy is a way to get the most beautiful diamond for your budget: put your money into cut, then pick a clarity grade that looks flawless to the naked eye rather than paying for perfection under magnification. Eye-clean means no inclusions are visible without magnification at a normal viewing distance.
entries.body:
Of the 4Cs, cut has the biggest effect on how a diamond actually looks. Cut is how well the stone's facets are proportioned and finished, and it governs how much light comes back to your eye as brightness, fire, and sparkle. A poorly cut diamond looks dull no matter how good its other grades are, while an excellently cut stone looks lively. The round brilliant, with its 57 or 58 facets, was worked out to return light this way, and the proportions Marcel Tolkowsky published in 1919 remain a reference point for it. So the first move is to prioritize cut quality and not compromise there.

The second move is to stop paying for clarity you cannot see. Clarity grades describe inclusions viewed under 10x magnification, but at normal viewing distance many stones graded below flawless show nothing to the naked eye. A stone like that is called eye-clean. Rather than buying the highest clarity grade, you find the grade at which a particular diamond is eye-clean and stop there. Two stones with the same grade can differ, since inclusions can sit under a prong or off to the side, so you evaluate the individual diamond, not just the letter on the report.

The same logic applies to color: a near-colorless stone that faces up white gives you the look without paying for the top color grade. The result is a diamond that is brilliant and clean to the eye, with your budget spent where the eye can actually tell the difference. This works identically for Lab Grown Diamonds, which are graded on the same scales.

sources.claim: The modern round brilliant has 57 or 58 facets, and its light-returning proportions trace to Marcel Tolkowsky's published analysis in Diamond Design.
Lab Grown Diamonds are real diamonds graded on the same scales as mined diamonds.

entries.definition: The Hope Diamond is a 45.52-carat deep grayish-blue diamond, colored by trace boron, on public display at the Smithsonian's National Museum of Natural History in Washington.
entries.body:
The Hope Diamond is a 45.52 carat deep grayish-blue diamond, described by the Smithsonian as the largest known deep-blue diamond. It has been on public display at the Smithsonian's National Museum of Natural History in Washington since the jeweler Harry Winston donated it in 1958. Winston famously sent it to the museum by registered mail.

Its blue color comes from tiny amounts of boron in the crystal. One striking property is that after exposure to ultraviolet light the stone briefly glows, or phosphoresces, a fiery red. The Hope also has a long, documented history. Its color and size trace back to a large blue diamond that the French merchant Jean-Baptiste Tavernier brought from India and sold to King Louis XIV in 1668, a stone recut into the French crown gem known as the French Blue. That gem disappeared during the French Revolution. In 2005 the Smithsonian published research concluding that the Hope Diamond was cut from the missing French Blue.

You will often hear that the diamond is cursed. That part is a legend, a story popularized by publicity in the early twentieth century rather than a documented fact, and the museum itself treats it as folklore. What makes the stone remarkable is real enough without it: an unusually large blue diamond, a rare boron-blue color, and a paper trail running back more than three centuries.

sources.claim: The Hope Diamond weighs 45.52 carats, is a deep grayish-blue color described by the Smithsonian as the largest known deep-blue diamond, and is displayed at the Smithsonian's National Museum of Natural History.
sources.source: Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History

sources.claim: Jeweler Harry Winston donated the Hope Diamond to the Smithsonian in 1958, sending it by registered mail.
The diamond's blue color is caused by trace boron, and the stone phosphoresces red after exposure to ultraviolet light.
sources.source: Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History

sources.claim: The Hope traces to a large blue diamond Jean-Baptiste Tavernier sold to Louis XIV in 1668 (recut as the "French Blue"), which vanished during the French Revolution; 2005 Smithsonian research concluded the Hope was cut from that gem.
sources.source: Smithsonian Institution (2005 research led by Jeffrey Post and Steven Attaway); History of the Hope Diamond

sources.claim: The "curse" of the Hope Diamond is a legend popularized by early twentieth-century publicity, not a documented fact.

entries.definition: The idea that an engagement ring should cost a set share of your income (one, two, or three months' salary) is not an old tradition or etiquette rule. It comes from De Beers advertising, and you are free to spend whatever fits your budget.
entries.body:
There is no traditional rule that a ring must cost one, two, or three months of your income. The guideline comes from De Beers advertising, produced by the agency N.W. Ayer from the late 1930s onward, and it was written to give shoppers a number to aim for. The specific figure shifted over time: a one month benchmark appeared in the middle of the century, and "two months' salary" became the familiar United States version by the 1980s. Advertising in other countries used other numbers.

Knowing this should take pressure off, not add it. The rule was never etiquette handed down through generations. It was a helpful-sounding target in an advertisement, and you are not doing anything wrong by spending more or less. A sensible way to choose is to set a budget you are comfortable with first, then get the best combination of the 4Cs within it, where cut usually does the most visible work. The ring is a personal, meaningful object, not a test you pass by hitting a dollar figure. Buy the piece that fits your life and your finances, and let the number in the old advertisement stay in the old advertisement.

sources.claim: The guideline to spend a fixed share of salary on an engagement ring originated in De Beers advertising created by N.W. Ayer, from the late 1930s onward, rather than in older tradition or etiquette.
sources.source: Task anchor (months-of-salary guideline, De Beers / N.W. Ayer advertising, 1930s onward)

sources.claim: A one-month-salary figure appeared in the mid twentieth century, and "two months' salary" became the familiar United States benchmark by the 1980s.
sources.source: De Beers advertising histories (press and trade accounts)

entries.definition: A three-stone ring sets a center diamond between two side stones. Its popular "past, present, future" meaning comes from a De Beers campaign around the year 2000, developed by the advertising agency J. Walter Thompson.
entries.body:
A three-stone ring is what it sounds like: a center diamond flanked by two side stones, usually set in a symmetrical row. It is a classic, versatile look, and the side stones can echo the center shape or contrast with it.

The familiar meaning attached to it, that the three diamonds stand for the past, present, and future of a relationship, is not ancient symbolism. It comes from a De Beers marketing campaign around the year 2000, developed by the advertising agency J. Walter Thompson, which promoted the three-stone ring as an anniversary and milestone piece. The "past, present, future" line gave the design an easy, appealing story, and it stuck.

Knowing the origin does not cheapen the idea. Plenty of couples like the framing precisely because it maps onto a real relationship: where you have been, where you are, and where you are going. Others simply like the balance of three stones across the finger. Either reason is valid. If you are choosing one, the decisions are ordinary jewelry ones: pick side stones proportionate to the center, matched in color, and set securely, since a design with more stones and prongs gives you a little more to keep an eye on over time. The symbolism is optional. The style stands on its own.

sources.claim: The "past, present, future" meaning of the three-stone diamond ring comes from a De Beers marketing campaign developed by the agency J. Walter Thompson around the year 2000, which promoted the three-stone ring as an anniversary and milestone piece.
sources.source: Jewelry and advertising trade reporting on the De Beers / J. Walter Thompson three-stone campaign (JCK Online; diamond-marketing histories)

entries.definition: A diamond tester is a handheld probe that measures how fast a stone carries heat away, exploiting the fact that diamond conducts heat better than almost any other material. Because a Lab Grown Diamond is diamond, it reads as diamond on these testers; the tools confirm the material, not its origin.
entries.body:
Diamond conducts heat exceptionally well, better than any other gemstone and better than nearly any bulk material at room temperature. A classic diamond tester presses a warm metal tip against the stone and measures how quickly heat drains from the tip. Diamond pulls heat away fast and gives a positive reading, while most simulants do not.

Two things follow, and both matter.

First, a Lab Grown Diamond passes as diamond because it is diamond. It has the same crystal structure and the same thermal conductivity as a mined stone, so a thermal tester cannot tell a Lab Grown Diamond from a mined one, and it is not designed to. It answers the question is this diamond, not where did this diamond come from.

Second, moissanite is the classic exception. Silicon carbide also conducts heat well, well enough to trigger a diamond reading on an older thermal-only tester. The fix is electrical. Moissanite conducts electricity and diamond generally does not, so modern combination testers measure both thermal and electrical conductivity and separate the two reliably.

A tester is a helpful screening tool, not a full identification. It can confirm that a stone is diamond and can flag a simulant, but telling a natural diamond from a Lab Grown Diamond calls for laboratory instruments such as spectroscopy, used by grading labs like GIA and IGI. Think of the handheld tester as a quick first check and a full grading report as the complete answer.

sources.claim: A Lab Grown Diamond is the same material as a mined diamond (the FTC defines a diamond by composition), so it registers as diamond on a thermal diamond tester.
sources.source: FTC, Guides for the Jewelry, Precious Metals, and Pewter Industries

entries.term: Tolkowsky and the modern round brilliant
entries.definition: Marcel Tolkowsky's 1919 study "Diamond Design" set out the round brilliant proportions that still guide how the shape is cut and judged. The modern round brilliant has 57 facets, or 58 counting the culet.
entries.body:
Marcel Tolkowsky belonged to a Belgian diamond-cutting family and trained as an engineer. In 1919 he published "Diamond Design," a study of how light reflects and refracts inside a diamond, and used it to propose the proportions that would return the most brightness and fire from a round stone. Those proportions became the reference point for what is often called the ideal, or American ideal, round brilliant.

The modern round brilliant has 57 facets, or 58 if you count the tiny flat facet at the bottom called the culet. Tolkowsky did not invent the round brilliant. The brilliant style had evolved over centuries of cutting; what he added was mathematics, giving cutters a target for table size, crown angle, and pavilion angle that balances brilliance and fire. More than a century later, his work is still the starting point for how a round diamond's cut is judged, and modern cut-grading systems descend from the same idea: that geometry, not luck, decides how a diamond handles light.

The practical lesson for a shopper is that cut is the quality you can see most directly. Two round diamonds of the same carat, color, and clarity can look noticeably different if one is cut to well-balanced proportions and the other is not. That is the tradition Tolkowsky started: treating the round brilliant as a solvable problem in light.

sources.claim: Marcel Tolkowsky, of a Belgian diamond-cutting family, trained as an engineer (a Doctor of Science in engineering from the University of London) and published "Diamond Design" in 1919, proposing optimized proportions for the round brilliant cut.
sources.source: Task anchor (Tolkowsky proportions, 1919); Cape Town Diamond Museum; GIA

sources.claim: The modern round brilliant cut has 57 facets, or 58 counting the culet.
sources.source: Task anchor (round brilliant 57-58 facets); standard gemology

sources.claim: Tolkowsky's specified proportions for table size, crown angle, and pavilion angle became the reference for the "ideal" round brilliant and underpin modern round-brilliant cut evaluation.
sources.source: Marcel Tolkowsky, "Diamond Design" (1919); GIA and diamond-trade references

entries.definition: Total internal reflection is what happens when light inside a dense material meets the boundary with a less dense material at a large enough angle from the perpendicular: instead of passing through, all of it reflects back inside. It is the mechanism that keeps light bouncing inside a diamond and returns it to the eye.
entries.body:
When light inside a stone reaches a facet, it can either pass out through the surface (refract) or bounce back in (reflect). Which one happens depends on the angle. Below a certain angle, called the critical angle, light escapes. At or beyond the critical angle, none of it escapes and all of it reflects. That complete reflection is total internal reflection.

The critical angle is set by the refractive index. The higher the index, the smaller the critical angle, and the easier it is to trap light. Diamond's high index of about 2.417 gives a critical angle of roughly 24.4 degrees, measured from the line perpendicular to the facet. Because that angle is small, most light that enters a diamond strikes the pavilion facets at an angle beyond it and reflects rather than escapes, often bouncing twice off the pavilion before heading back up through the table.

This is why cut proportions decide how bright a diamond looks. The pavilion facets have to be angled so that light coming down through the crown meets them beyond the critical angle and reflects back up. If the pavilion is too shallow or too deep, light meets the facets below the critical angle, passes through, and leaks out the bottom, leaving dark spots. Total internal reflection is not automatic: the material makes it possible, but only a well-cut stone arranges its facets to use it fully.

The physics is identical for mined and Lab Grown Diamonds, since both share the same refractive index. The same principle runs fiber optics, where light is trapped inside a glass fiber and reflected along its length.

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically identical to a mined diamond, so its refractive index and critical angle are the same.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: Hardness is a material's resistance to being scratched; toughness is its resistance to chipping, cracking, or breaking. They are different properties, and a stone can rank high on one and only moderately on the other.
entries.body:
People use hard loosely to mean durable, but gemology keeps two ideas apart. Hardness is about the surface: can something scratch it? That is what the Mohs scale measures. Toughness is about the whole stone: hit it or drop it, and does it chip or fracture? A material can be extremely hard and still not especially tough.

Diamond is the clearest example. It is the hardest natural material, a 10 on Mohs, so in normal wear almost nothing scratches it. But diamond has perfect cleavage in four directions, meaning its atomic structure has planes along which it splits cleanly. A sharp knock at the wrong angle, especially on an exposed corner or a pointed tip, can chip or cleave a diamond even though nothing could scratch it. Hard, yes. Indestructible, no.

At the other end, some less hard materials are remarkably tough. Jade, meaning nephrite and jadeite, is only about 6 to 7 on Mohs, so it scratches relatively easily, yet its interlocking fibrous structure makes it extraordinarily resistant to breaking. It has been carved and even used for tools for exactly that reason.

For a ring you actually wear, both properties matter. Hardness keeps the surface bright and unscratched over years. Toughness governs how it survives a hard bump. This is also why protective setting design and sensible everyday care are good practice for any fine gemstone, including diamond. That is caring for the piece well, not a sign that anything is wrong with the stone.

entries.definition: An ultrasonic cleaner uses high-frequency sound waves to shake dirt off jewelry in a liquid bath. It is very effective on many plain diamond solitaires, but it can loosen stones and stress included, treated, or fracture-filled diamonds, so use it with care.
entries.body:
An ultrasonic cleaner sends high-frequency sound waves through a water-and-detergent bath. Those waves create microscopic bubbles that collapse against whatever is in the tank, a process called cavitation, and that scrubbing action lifts grime from tight spaces a brush cannot reach. It is the same principle jewelers use at the counter.

For a well-set, untreated diamond solitaire, an ultrasonic is usually safe and works well. The caution comes from three situations. First, vibration can wiggle a stone loose if the prongs are already worn or the setting is not tight, so it is not a good idea to run one right before you have had the mounting checked. Second, a diamond with a significant inclusion that reaches the surface, such as a feather (a small internal fracture), can be stressed by cavitation, and in rare cases an existing feather can widen. Third, some diamonds are fracture-filled or otherwise treated, and the energy and heat can disturb that treatment.

Because Lab Grown Diamonds are grown crystals with the same physical properties as mined ones, the rules are identical for both: the concern is the inclusions and the setting, not the origin. If you are unsure whether your stone is heavily included or treated, or whether the setting is tight, skip the machine and use the warm-soapy-water method instead, or let your jeweler run it and check the prongs at the same time. When you do use an ultrasonic at home, run it briefly and do not crowd several pieces together where they can knock into each other.

sources.claim: Lab Grown Diamonds are real diamonds with the same physical and optical properties as mined diamonds.

related: Cleaning a Diamond Ring at Home

entries.definition: A V-tip prong, also called a V-prong, is a prong bent into a V shape that cups and shields the pointed corner of a diamond. It is used on any shape that ends in a point, where the tip is the most fragile spot.
entries.body:
A V-tip prong wraps two sides of a stone's corner at once, forming a protective sleeve around the point instead of a single claw pressing on it. This matters because a diamond is the hardest natural material, rated 10 on the Mohs scale, but hardness is resistance to scratching, not to breaking. A sharp point concentrates stress, so a knock in the wrong direction can chip it. The V-tip spreads and guards that vulnerable corner.

You will see V-tips on the pointed ends of marquise and pear cuts, on the tip of a heart, on all four corners of a princess (square modified brilliant) cut, and on the two pointed ends of a Dutch Marquise, a trade name for an elongated hexagonal cut diamond with pointed ends and straight angular sides. Emerald and radiant cuts have clipped, blunter corners and are usually held with ordinary prongs, though corner V-tips remain an option. When you are choosing a setting for a pointed shape, confirm the design includes V-tips at the points, because a plain prong on a bare tip is the most common way pointed stones get damaged. A good jeweler builds this in as a matter of course. Well-fitted V-tips also keep the point centered and symmetrical, which preserves the crisp outline that gives shapes like the marquise and the Dutch Marquise their length.
