sources.claim: Diamond rates 10, the maximum, on the Mohs scale of scratch hardness; Mohs hardness measures resistance to scratching, not toughness against breakage.
sources.source: Mohs scale of mineral hardness (introduced by Friedrich Mohs)

entries.definition: Vena amoris, Latin for "vein of love," is the old legend that a vein runs straight from the ring finger to the heart. It is a myth, since no such special vein exists, but it remains a charming reason for the ring-finger custom.
entries.body:
"Vena amoris" is Latin for "vein of love." The legend holds that a single vein runs straight from the fourth finger, the ring finger, to the heart, and that this is why the wedding or engagement ring is worn there, on the left hand in much of the Western world. It is a lovely story, and it is a story. There is no unique vein linking that one finger to the heart. The fingers all share a similar network of veins that return blood to the heart, a system first described in detail by the physician William Harvey in 1628.

The idea itself is old. A version of it appears in the writing of the Roman author Macrobius around the year 400, and the Latin phrase turns up in later marriage texts, including a treatise by Henry Swinburne published in 1686. So the belief predates any modern jeweler by many centuries. It was never a sales invention.

None of this should spoil the custom. Knowing that the anatomy is a myth takes nothing away from the meaning. People have chosen the ring finger for a very long time, and the act of placing a ring there carries whatever significance you and your partner give it. The vein was never the point. The promise is.

sources.claim: "Vena amoris" (Latin for "vein of love") names the belief that a vein runs from the ring finger directly to the heart, long cited as a reason for wearing the ring on that finger.
sources.source: Vena amoris, Wikipedia (encyclopedic summary of the tradition)

sources.claim: A version of the belief is recorded by the Roman writer Macrobius around 400 AD, and the Latin phrase appears in later marriage writing, including Henry Swinburne's treatise published in 1686.
sources.source: Vena amoris, Wikipedia (citing Macrobius and Henry Swinburne)

sources.claim: Blood circulation, by which veins in all fingers return blood to the heart, was described by the physician William Harvey in 1628, contradicting the idea of a single vein unique to the ring finger.
sources.source: William Harvey, "De Motu Cordis" (history of medicine)

entries.definition: Verifying a grading report means confirming the certificate is genuine and that it actually describes the diamond in front of you. The three-step check is: look the report up on the lab's own website, match the laser inscription on the stone to the report number, and confirm the measurements match.
entries.body:
A diamond grading report is a document from an independent laboratory describing a stone's carat weight, measurements, color, clarity, and cut. Verifying it is a simple habit that protects every buyer, and good sellers expect it and make it easy. It comes down to three steps.

Step one: look up the report. Major labs publish an online report-check tool. Enter the report number on the lab's own website (GIA Report Check or IGI's Verify Your Report page, for example) and confirm the details on screen match the document you were given. This confirms the report is real and has not been altered.

Step two: match the stone to the report. Most modern reports record a laser inscription, a tiny copy of the report number etched onto the diamond's girdle, which is the thin outer edge. Under magnification, or with the seller's help, read that inscription and confirm it matches the report number. This ties this specific certificate to this specific stone.

Step three: confirm the measurements. Check that the physical stone's millimeter dimensions and carat weight line up with what the report lists. Measurements act like a fingerprint, and a match closes the loop.

One note on labs for Lab Grown Diamonds. IGI issues full 4C reports on Lab Grown stones. As of October 1, 2025, GIA moved its Lab Grown reports to descriptive Premium and Standard tiers rather than the traditional letter and number grades, and HRD Antwerp has announced it will stop grading loose Lab Grown Diamonds in 2026. Knowing which lab issued a report, and what that lab reports today, is part of reading it well.

sources.claim: GIA moved its Lab Grown reports to Premium and Standard tiers instead of traditional 4C grades.
IGI continues to issue full 4C grading reports for Lab Grown Diamonds.
HRD Antwerp announced in 2025 that it will stop grading loose Lab Grown Diamonds in 2026.

entries.definition: White gold is gold alloyed with white metals such as palladium, nickel, silver, and zinc to lighten its color, then usually coated with a thin layer of rhodium for a bright, mirror-white finish. That rhodium plating wears off over time and needs to be reapplied periodically.
entries.body:
White gold starts as the same gold as yellow gold, alloyed to the same karats, so 14k is 58.3 percent gold and 18k is 75 percent, but the metals mixed in are chosen to lighten the color rather than warm it. Even so, most white gold has a faint warm or grayish tint on its own, because it still contains a large share of naturally yellow gold. To get the bright, cool white that people expect, jewelers plate the finished piece with rhodium.

Rhodium is a rare, hard, brilliantly white metal in the platinum group. A micro-thin rhodium coating gives white gold its crisp shine and adds a hard protective surface. The key thing to understand, and to plan for, is that this plating is a surface layer, not the color of the metal underneath. With daily wear it gradually rubs off, most noticeably on the underside of the band and other high-contact spots, letting the slightly warmer gold beneath show through. This is completely normal and not a defect. The fix is simple: a jeweler cleans and re-plates the ring, and it looks new again. How often depends on the wearer, but roughly once a year to once every few years is typical, and it is an inexpensive routine service. If you would rather never replate, platinum or yellow gold avoids the issue, since their color is intrinsic. White gold offers a bright, modern white look and good hardness, and because it is less dense and easier to work than platinum, it usually costs less to make. Some white gold uses nickel, which can irritate nickel-sensitive skin, so palladium-based white gold or platinum is the alternative there.

entries.definition: White sapphire is colorless corundum, a crystalline form of aluminum oxide (Al2O3), used as a durable diamond alternative. It is the same mineral species as ruby and blue sapphire, just without the trace elements that give those stones their color.
entries.body:
Sapphire is the gem name for corundum in any color except red, since red corundum is called ruby. When corundum forms with essentially no coloring impurities it is colorless, and that is white sapphire. It occurs in nature and is also commonly grown in laboratories, and both are the same material.

White sapphire's main strength as a diamond alternative is durability. At Mohs 9 it is the third-hardest common gem material, behind diamond at 10 and moissanite at about 9.25, and it holds a polish and resists scratching far better than softer simulants like cubic zirconia. That hardness, combined with a colorless body, is why it appears in engagement rings as an alternative center stone or as accent stones.

Where white sapphire differs from diamond is in the way it handles light. Its refractive index is lower, about 1.76, and its dispersion is very low, about 0.018, well under diamond's 0.044. In plain terms it has little fire and a softer, more glassy brightness rather than the sharp sparkle of a well-cut diamond. It is also doubly refractive. None of this is a defect. It is simply the honest optical signature of corundum. A white sapphire is a real gemstone, sold as corundum, and a fair seller never presents it as a diamond.

entries.definition: Light leakage is light that enters a diamond and escapes through the pavilion instead of returning to the eye, leaving dull or dark areas. Windowing is the extreme case: a see-through zone, usually under the table, where light passes straight through so you can see out the back of the stone.
entries.body:
Both come from the same cause: facets that meet light below the critical angle, so the light refracts out through the bottom rather than reflecting back by total internal reflection. Where a well-cut stone traps light and sends it back up, a poorly proportioned one lets it slip away.

Pavilion depth is the main lever. If the pavilion is too shallow, light strikes the bottom facets too flatly, passes through, and the center of the stone becomes a window you can nearly see through to whatever sits behind it. Shallow pavilions in round brilliants also produce a fish-eye, a gray ring under the table that is a reflection of the girdle. If the pavilion is too deep, light reflects across and escapes out the far side, and the center looks dark, a defect called a nailhead. Leakage can also come from a misaligned culet, an off-center table, or poor symmetry.

You can spot leakage with the naked eye as flat or lifeless patches, and more precisely with an Idealscope, where leakage shows up as white areas instead of returned red, or with an ASET map. Checking for windowing and leakage is standard, sensible practice when judging a cut, whatever the seller.

Some leakage is normal and even useful, since a little contrast reads as sparkle. The problem is large or centered leakage that steals brightness from the part of the stone you look at most. Leakage is purely a function of cut geometry and the diamond material, so it behaves the same in mined and Lab Grown Diamonds, and it is most fully mapped for round brilliants, where the ideal angles are well defined.

sources.claim: The ASET (Angular Spectrum Evaluation Tool), developed by AGS Laboratories, and the Idealscope reveal returned light versus leakage and contrast, showing leakage as white or uncolored areas.
sources.source: AGS Laboratories (American Gem Society); PriceScope education, ASET / Idealscope

sources.claim: Under the U.S. FTC Jewelry Guides, a Lab Grown Diamond is a real diamond, chemically and physically the same material as a mined diamond, so light leakage behaves identically.
sources.source: U.S. Federal Trade Commission, revised Jewelry Guides

entries.definition: Yellow gold is gold alloyed with metals such as copper and silver to make it hard enough for jewelry, with purity measured in karats out of 24. 18k gold is 75 percent gold, 14k is about 58 percent, and 24k is pure gold.
entries.body:
Pure gold, which is 24 karat, is too soft to hold a diamond securely or survive daily wear, so jewelry gold is an alloy: gold mixed with other metals to add strength. Karat measures how much of the mix is pure gold, in twenty-fourths, and the math is simple and uncontested. 24k is 24 parts out of 24, or 100 percent gold. 18k is 18 parts out of 24, which is 75 percent gold and 25 percent other metals. 14k is 14 out of 24, about 58.3 percent gold. 10k is 10 out of 24, about 41.7 percent.

In yellow gold, the added metals are chiefly copper and silver, balanced to keep a warm yellow color. Higher karat means richer color and more pure gold, but also a softer, more scratch-prone metal and a higher price. Lower karat is harder and paler in color. For engagement rings, 14k and 18k are the usual choices: 14k for hardness and a friendlier price, 18k for a deeper color and higher gold content. A real advantage of yellow gold is that it never needs the replating that white gold requires, because its color runs all the way through the metal, so its warmth is permanent with normal care. It suits every diamond shape and can look especially warm against a range of skin tones. A jeweler can resize yellow gold readily, which makes it a forgiving, practical choice, and it pairs naturally with warmer-toned diamonds.
